- **References:** `<5h78qqF3hguocU1@mid.individual.net> <4MWdnUDRHLIlPTPbnZ2dnUVZ_h2pnZ2d@comcast.com> <5h97ekF3hv8npU1@mid.individual.net> <1185913066.280005.293910@i13g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <5h9tuqF3ja9auU1@mid.individual.net> <1185941885.429033.151800@e9g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <Rjasi.24367$GO6.1354@newsfe21.lga> <1186019993.417899.323910@x35g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <ERmsi.148737$wG2.108969@newsfe17.lga> <5hfn3qF3kjtfmU1@mid.individual.net> <D5Ksi.30444$GO6.4640@newsfe21.lga> <prt6b31bebbhccie1hpi9fe87dskajfpq8@4ax.com>`

```
On Aug 4, 6:52 am, Howard Brazee <how...@brazee.net> wrote:
> On Fri, 3 Aug 2007 13:01:37 -0500, "tlmfru" <la...@mts.net> wrote:
> > Humour invariably
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> >haven't decided yet why they are so funny).

> Aha moments can be funny - seeing a different relationship than we
> expect.   The story about the boy who finds a dozen ways to measure
> the height of a building using a barometer is funny.   Again, it is a
> learning situation for us.

In an analysis of my reactions I see that laughter is usually caused
by creating a conflift in the brain and then resolving it. Puns are
usually only funny the first time they are used because they don't
subsequently create any conflict.

I find there is a general difference between american and british
comedy. Obviously there is a great deal of cross over, but typically a
good 'british' comedy will laugh at themselves while american comedy
may laugh at someone else.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ALTER design (Was: Code problems with Perform Thru Exit causes fall through)

_(thread depth: 23)_

- **From:** Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2007-08-03T18:50:42-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1186192242.156737.159010@q75g2000hsh.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1186184120.293135.98060@i13g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<5h78qqF3hguocU1@mid.individual.net> <4MWdnUDRHLIlPTPbnZ2dnUVZ_h2pnZ2d@comcast.com> <5h97ekF3hv8npU1@mid.individual.net> <1185913066.280005.293910@i13g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <5h9tuqF3ja9auU1@mid.individual.net> <1185941885.429033.151800@e9g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <Rjasi.24367$GO6.1354@newsfe21.lga> <1186019993.417899.323910@x35g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <ERmsi.148737$wG2.108969@newsfe17.lga> <5hfn3qF3kjtfmU1@mid.individual.net> <D5Ksi.30444$GO6.4640@newsfe21.lga> <prt6b31bebbhccie1hpi9fe87dskajfpq8@4ax.com> <1186184120.293135.98060@i13g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`

```
On 4 Aug, 00:35, Richard <rip...@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>
> In an analysis of my reactions I see that laughter is usually caused
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I may be out of date but my understanding of laughter is that it is a
nervous reaction induced by relief at NOT being the butt of the joke.
The rictus (or grin) produced in a laugh is similar to the rictus
produced by dogs and chimps when admitting subordination to a superior
in a conflict situation.

There is a difference in styles of comedy (US v UK). It is often noted
(on the east bank of the pond) that the UK comics can laugh at
themselves but that US comics don't. Also, whereas we are happy to
take the mickey out of the Queen (gawd bless 'er maj), the
establishment and almost anything else we can think of, the US comics
appear to be a little unwilling to lay into Bush et al.

Finally, I don't approve of UK comedies being redone in the US for
your domestic audience. It seems to me to lose something in the
translation (the Office is a classic example - it just is not funny
with American actors).
```

Alistair wrote:
> On 4 Aug, 03:14, LX-i <lxi0...@netscape.net> wrote:
>> Alistair wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> and refuse to get it.

Jay Leno - that's NBC's Tonight Show.  He'll pretty much rip whoever's 
in power.  He had fun with Clinton, he's had fun with Bush, and now that 
the Democrats have control of the House and Senate, he's having fun with 
that too.  (Of course, these days the Democrat jokes are writing 
themselves.)  Plus, the presidential race is always a wealth of material 
from both sides.

As Richard mentioned, Jon Stewart is another that's not afraid to rip 
whoever is in power.  Their war coverage for the Iraq war is 
"Mess-o-potamia".  I watch him occasionally - he is funny, and sadly, 
the current administration has given comedians lots of material.

This week, though, our Commander in Chief decided to become the 
"comedian in chief".  He was at a press conference, and here's how it 
went...

(begin quote)
At last week's press conference at Camp David, President George Bush 
insulted BBC political editor Nick Robinson, the Daily Mirror reports.

Robinson, who has asked Bush pointed questions in the past such as 
whether the president was ï¿½in denialï¿½ over the Iraq war, posed a 
question about whether he could trust visiting British Prime Minister 
Gordon Brown not to ï¿½cut and runï¿½ from Iraq.

Bush replied with a dismissal: ï¿½Are you still hanging around?ï¿½

Later on, Bush poked fun at the bare-pate of Robinson, joking, ï¿½Youï¿½d 
better cover up your bald head, itï¿½s getting hot out.ï¿½

The respected British reporter shot back, ï¿½I didnï¿½t know you cared.ï¿½

Bush responded with a cool, ï¿½I donï¿½t.ï¿½ The Mirror reports that Bush then 
ï¿½snorted disdainfullyï¿½ and ï¿½walked away to laughter.ï¿½
(end quote)

:)
```

> > On 4 Aug, 03:14, LX-i <lxi0...@netscape.net> wrote:

> Jay Leno - that's NBC's Tonight Show.

wrong host to the right show. I have seen Jay Leno on the Tonight Show
and the other one (whose name has slipped my mind and isn't on the
Tonight Show) too.

> He'll pretty much rip whoever's
> in power.  He had fun with Clinton, he's had fun with Bush, and now that
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

So whichever way (except for your last presidential elections) he is
pretty much on the winning side?

> As Richard mentioned, Jon Stewart is another that's not afraid to rip
> whoever is in power.  Their war coverage for the Iraq war is
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Regrettably, IMHO, not very humourous. I imagine Bush gave his usual
nervous-laugh-seeking-popular-approval complete with death rictus
leer?

Nick Robinson is one of our more mild reporters. Now if Bush came up
against Jeremy Paxman (Newsnight on BBC2) that would be a show worth
watching.

Alistair wrote:
> On 5 Aug, 01:20, LX-i <lxi0...@netscape.net> wrote:
>> Alistair wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Tonight Show) too.

David Letterman?  (He's on the Late Show on CBS.  He used to do "Late 
Night with David Letterman" on NBC, that came on after Johnny Carson's 
Tonight Show.  Him not getting the Tonight Show gig when Carson retired 
is what led him to CBS.  Now, Conan O'Brien (who still hasn't really 
grown on me - his stand-up is somewhat funny, but the other stuff not so 
much, although his is the show that brought us Triumph the Insult Comedy 
Dog) is scheduled to take over for Leno when he retires in a few years.

I can't believe I still know that stuff.  I'm usually in bed by 10pm 
these days.  :)

>> He'll pretty much rip whoever's
>> in power.  He had fun with Clinton, he's had fun with Bush, and now that
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> pretty much on the winning side?

Well, the person who wins obviously provides more material.  I'm sure 
the Blair-to-Brown joke ratio will be tipping more toward the Brown side 
now that he's the one in charge.  Think of them as equal opportunity 
mockers.

>> The respected British reporter shot back, "I didn't know you cared."
>>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> leer?

You don't find that a humorous exchange?  I don't know what Bush did - I 
haven't seen the video.

> Nick Robinson is one of our more mild reporters. Now if Bush came up
> against Jeremy Paxman (Newsnight on BBC2) that would be a show worth
> watching.

Heh - do they do pay-per-view over there?  That could be a money-maker!
```

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1186192242.156737.159010@q75g2000hsh.googlegroups.com...
> On 4 Aug, 00:35, Richard <rip...@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>>
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Sorry, Alistair, I have to strongly disagree.

As a fan of Ricky Gervais I need to point out he had overall authority on 
the American version as well as the British one. Having worked in an office 
block in Uxbridge and had lunch in Slough on numerous occasions, I related 
easily to the English one and found it excruciatingly funny. But, I also 
enjoyed the American one and it isn't true to say it wasn't funny; it was 
different, but still very funny.

I don't know if you have seen "Extras". I think this is like the fruition of 
Gervais' comic genius. The episodes with Orlando Bloom, Cate Blanchett, and 
Robert Lindsay/Robert De Niro, I think, are just priceless.

Still, I guess "funny", like "beauty", is in the eye of the beholder...

Pete
```

On 4 Aug, 04:28, "Pete Dashwood" <dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
wrote:
> "Alistair" <alist...@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Sorry, Alistair, I have to strongly disagree.

You are entitled to do so. I would point out that my sense of humour
is very different to most peoples' senses of humour.

>
> As a fan of Ricky Gervais

He makes me cringe. Unfortunately he has a more than passing
resemblance to myself and his self-deprecating approval-seeking style
does not endear him to me.

> I need to point out he had overall authority on
> the American version as well as the British one. Having worked in an office
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

There were some very funny moments in the Office but much of it (and
his stand-up shows) is cringe-inducing to me.

> I don't know if you have seen "Extras".

Yes, very, very funny. I bet it gets remade for the US with Tom Cruise
for the lead role.

> I think this is like the fruition of
> Gervais' comic genius. The episodes with Orlando Bloom, Cate Blanchett, and
> Robert Lindsay/Robert De Niro, I think, are just priceless.
>
> Still, I guess "funny", like "beauty", is in the eye of the beholder...

Very much so. I know people who find jokes about paedophilia funny. I
don't and I take the opportunity to correct them. Which reminds me,
Ricky Gervais sailed too close to the knuckle in one of his stand-up
shows by using a paedophilia joke (something about his grandfather).
```

Ritter etc. in it) which not only took the premise but even cribbed the

don't think it aired more that a couple of episodes.  It was appalling!

PL

```
>>> On 8/3/2007 at 7:50 PM, in message
<1186192242.156737.159010@q75g2000hsh.googlegroups.com>,
Alistair<alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> On 4 Aug, 00:35, Richard <rip...@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> with American actors).

Prior to the American version of The Office I had seen only one of the
original version.  I didn't like it.  I love the American version.  Since
it's been airing I've seen maybe six more of the original version episodes. 
I still prefer the American version.  Michael Scott has his problems, but
he's often likable.  David Brent is simply a jerk!  Yes, I know he's
supposed to be, but I just don't like it.  In fact, almost all of the
characters in the original are not very likable; even Tim can be a bit of a
jerk.  And Dawn is simply a doormat.  Pam is much better.

Just my opinion.  I do like Extras, though.  (Which isn't to say I think it
should be redone for a U.S. audience.  I think that The Office is one of the
rare times that has actually worked.)

I also like a lot the new show Flight of the Conchords, from New Zealand. 
(Well, not sure if it can be considered 'from New Zealand', since it's
filmed in the U.S., but...).  Reminds me somewhat of Extras; especially
Murray (the band's manager) being somewhat like the agent in Extras.  Only
not quite a dim.)

Frank
```

On Mon, 6 Aug 2007 11:02:04 -0600, "Frank Swarbrick"
<Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> wrote:

>Prior to the American version of The Office I had seen only one of the
>original version.  I didn't like it.  I love the American version.  Since
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>jerk.  And Dawn is simply a doormat.  Pam is much better.

In general, I want my bad guys to want to be good.   I didn't like
Frank in M*A*S*H, but liked Winchester.    Frank was just a
caricature, Winchester was a real person.

And at the risk of invoking Goodwin - I think it is *dangerous* for
the media to continue portraying Hitler as a clown instead of as a
charismatic leader that could happen to us.
```

"When I die
I may not get to Heaven
'Cos I don't know
if they let cowboys in.
If they don't,
just let me go to TEXAS!
'Cos Texas is as close as I've bin..."

(I always hear a mental "Yeeeeeeeeehaarrrrrrrrrrr" whenever I think about 
this, and I'm smiling even now as I write it here :-))

The ability to laugh at ourselves and our culture is essential, if we are 
not to descend into pomposity and pretentiousness.

Fortunately, this ability is by no means the preserve only of the British.

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ALTER design (Was: Code problems with Perform Thru Exit causes fall through)

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-08-01T13:28:33+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5ha5u2F3i8a3jU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1185223084.744466.291540@22g2000hsm.googlegroups.com> <1185565019.612580.222200@e9g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <eWrqi.35$IE5.12@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net> <qKvqi.105614$wG2.60480@newsfe17.lga> <1185584321.018963.77560@e9g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <13alet62ejv7f91@corp.supernews.com> <1185663930.742550.293780@z24g2000prh.googlegroups.com> <13ao1utdpul1cc@corp.supernews.com> <5h3djvF3imk7tU1@mid.individual.net> <3i3sa3hcmfal9nepkknnke9cq2eu4981c5@4ax.com> <5h78qqF3hguocU1@mid.individual.net> <4MWdnUDRHLIlPTPbnZ2dnUVZ_h2pnZ2d@comcast.com>`

```
"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message
news:4MWdnUDRHLIlPTPbnZ2dnUVZ_h2pnZ2d@comcast.com...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Cheeky monkey :-)!

> My first experience with the ALTER verb was our shop standards when I
> first got there.  "The use of ALTER is strictly forbidden."  :)  I
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Now, see, that's a really good question. So much more interesting than
simply arguing about the relative merits of ALTER.:-)

I'll attempt to give you an answer that excludes the things already covered,
like first time thru switches, although these are worth looking at for a
moment, in passing...

If you put an IF statement at the start of  a piece of code (maybe even a
SECTION... :-)) that says:

IF FIRST-TIME-THRU
     SET NOT-FIRST-TIME-THRU TO TRUE
     PERFORM TAKE-THE-LID-OFF
END-IF
PERFORM POUR-A-MEASURE
...

And this section gets executed  (by PERFORM or GO TO) 10 million times, you
are doing a compare that can never be true 9,999,999 times. Nowadays, that
isn't such a big deal, but there was a time when it was...

If, instead, you were to formulate it as:

FIRST-TIME.
      GO TO REMOVE-LID.
REMOVE-LID.
       ALTER FIRST-TIME TO PROCEED TO OTHER-TIMES
       PERFORM TAKE-THE-LID-OFF.
OTHER-TIMES.
       PERFORM POUR-A-MEASURE
...

You have removed 9,999,999 instructions from the execution. Now it just does
a branch instead of a compare. On most platforms this is the quickest
instruction in the set. (Almost invariably, a single machine instruction).
Compares on some machines can be horrific, generating up to a dozen machine
instructions (especially if data conversion is required before the compare
can take place.)

Can you honestly say that the second example above is much more complex than
the first? Or so "horrendous" it couldn't be maintained by a trained COBOL
Programmer? :-)

It doesn't even take much more writing, yet it saves 9,999,999 compare
instructions, and is maybe 12 times more efficient than your IF switch....

Some people would call that "good" :-)

YOUR QUESTION:

> Now my curiosity is more theoretical.  How does one think to design ALTER
> in a good way?

It isn't really about designing ALTER, it is about approaching things in a
different way.

I'll rephrase your question thus:

"So, what kind of mindset do you need to deal with COBOL that
modifies itself as it runs?"

MY ANSWER:

Something close to the old Assembler mindset where instructions in memory
were frequently modified (not just the Branch instructions) to save time and
space.

You need a non-linear mindset that is imaginative and intelligent. What
DeBono calls "Lateral" thinking,  but not only must you think "outside the
box" you must do it "on the hop".   Some people really enjoy this way of
working and are very good at it, most do not and are not... It is definitely
not for the faint-hearted...(That's what I meant when I said "the wimps won
the day") :-)

Now let's look at a few cases where the ALTER solution is probably the most
elegant one available...

(Most of these CAN be implemented without using ALTER, but, in my opinion, 
implementing them WITH ALTER actually added significant value.)

Consider a dynamic process where a series of  "stages" are executed in a
variable sequence, but only as the execution proceeds, can the subsequent
stage requirements be decided. Normally, we would write a program that
controlled the flow, tracked each item through each stage,  and could cater
for every possible "path". That would be linear.

But what about when the "path" is actually a network and the possibilities
are approaching infinity? We have little choice but to cause the stages to
be switched as we proceed through them. "Fast switching"...rings a bell...
sure there used to be something in COBOL that could do that...Oops! Not any
more... :-)

What about a state transition processor?  You have a bunch of, say, 700 odd
keywords for COBOL and you need to parse a COBOL source program. You COULD
lookup every word the parser encountered against the Reserved word list.
That would be linear. (It would also be pretty slow).

Suppose instead you had a series of transition diagrams that showed the 
states you are interested in. Suppose further you had a series of "State 
Evaluation" engines  (Modules that decide what the current letter of the 
word is and how this relates from context, to something we may or may not be 
interested in pursuing. These modules need to dynamically switch the 
sequence of processing, depending on what the current  state within the 
current context is. It's a bit too complex to go into detail here (I wrote 
an article about it many years ago, but it is on a different computer in an 
attic somewhere.) )

Anyway, perhaps this very general description is enough for you to get the 
idea. (I hasten to add that such processes do NOT get maintained in source 
code (apart from maybe adding new state/context process sequences when new 
Reserved words are added to the language)  The solution described is still 
linear, but it is dynamically linear and, again, requires fast switching 
within a given context.
(This particular one can be implemented without ALTER by a slightly modified 
approach, but ALTER does it faster.)

Consider a series of "parameters" that arrive independently (or not) from 
different sources,and conflict with each other. (This is non-linear)You 
start the process then realise it has just been made redundant by a new 
conflicting parameter set. So, it needs to be dynamically 
modified...Otherwise, what has been done so far is wasted. You'd wait until 
all the paremeter sets have arrived before starting? What if you can't do 
that? Suppose there is no way of  knowing if or when these things may 
arrive? Suppose the process is driving machines that cannot be switched off 
without requiring expensive restarts? It's a dynamic process that needs a 
dynamic solution. ALTER is a very good way to dynamically modify a process 
chain, even if it is already under way.

What about a railway network with  various sets of points? (I find this a 
very helpful way to visualize systems that have ALTERs in them).  In order 
to get to a specific destination you need to change point settings. You 
could do this before you start (if you know what the destination is and it 
won't change when you are on the journey), or, you might need to keep your 
options open and accept any one of several destinations, depending on what 
happens en route.

If you go to Europe and are in say, England, you can book train  travel to 
almost any European destination from almost any station in the U.K. 
Alternate routings are provided instantly and the system talks to mainframes 
and client/server networks all over Europe. This is achieved by a ticketing 
system which was developed to coincide with the opening of the Channel 
Tunnel. It is called TRIBUTE and I worked on it. The English part of it is 
written in MicroFocus COBOL and uses dynamic switching to book each leg of a 
journey. A journey can be "defined" simply by giving values for each of the 
"switch points" in the network. (in effect, ALTERing them as the journey 
progresses) . Each node will then do the ticketing for that country/region 
It is totally flexible and dynamic.

If you think about a process that is like the described railway network and 
has, say, 10 "switching points", each of which is two-states (in reality, 
processes can switch to many more than 2 destinations, but this will do just 
to stimulate thought), then there are 2 **10 possible ways through the 
process. Would you code 2 ** 10 IF statements...? I don't think so. Each 
item to be processed is "defined" with a "pack" of 10 ALTER statements. 
These are executed and then the process is run. (You might think of mice 
running through an adjustable maze that has 10 doors in it; you set the 
doors how you want them, then release the mouse :-))

So these are just some of the cases where ability to modify a process 
dynamically can be considered "useful".

I'm not suggesting we should bring back ALTER (although I wouldn't mind if 
it was re-instated), I think I am just mourning the fact that programming 
gets "dumbed down" to the lowest common denominator because some people are 
not capable of handling power tools.
(These are usually the same ones who bleat about "ease of maintenance", 
then never document what they write or format it so it is readable...)

(I'm sure most people reading this have worked on sites with restrictive 
programming standards that were brought in because somebody couldn't follow 
something, or somebody thought that "complex" constructs might be 
dangerous...It is the same syndrome; if a dummy can do it we are all that 
much safer... Perish the thought that people might be taught or trained how 
to safely use power tools...)

Even if I never used it, I'd like to think the tool was available, in case I 
DID need it.

Solutions are often proscribed by the tools we use (Whether they SHOULD be 
or not is a subject for a different discussion...)

I've just accepted a job that involves writing some more powerful tools for 
a Database conversion.  Without these tools it would be two years work. With 
the tools, hopefully, not more than 6 months. Time to write the tools? 
Around one month. (I'll assist with using them as well... so I'm committed 
to this for more than a month :-)) The point is that tools empower us and 
enable us to reduce time, money, and effort. (I can't imagine how I got by 
without VS 2005; this tool has increased my productivity at least threefold, 
but it is hard to quantify or prove.) Removing tools from the box is always 
sad in my book. Doing it because of a "Nanny" mentality just sucks...

OK, Daniel, I hope you might review your feelings about ALTER (a construct 
you have probably never been allowed to use :-)). As you can see there is at 
least one person (actually, I'm reminded that Howard never had a problem 
with it either so I'm sure there are more, but I can't speak for them :-)) 
who never got unwrapped about ALTER, or spaghetti programming, or SECTIONs, 
or GO TOs, but simply saw it all as part of the "job". Different styles, 
some "better", some "worse", but realizing that these words are totally 
subjective.

Got some convoluted  code you can't figure out? Give it to the 
professionals.

It's only COBOL... :-)

Pete.
```

In article <5ha5u2F3i8a3jU1@mid.individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

[snip]

>I'm not suggesting we should bring back ALTER (although I wouldn't mind if 
>it was re-instated), I think I am just mourning the fact that programming 
>gets "dumbed down" to the lowest common denominator because some people are 
>not capable of handling power tools.

Mr Dashwood, it just might possibly be that enough people have shown that 
their skills with 'power tools' are so slipshod that they wind up causing 
more harm than good; restricting the use can then be seen as a simple 
business decision, risk versus benefit... this is all being done for an 
entity which is in existence to make profit.

[snip]

>(I'm sure most people reading this have worked on sites with restrictive 
>programming standards that were brought in because somebody couldn't follow 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>to safely use power tools...)

Leaving aside what seems to be a recent rediscovery of Heidegger's 'Das 
Mann' formulation... the theory - and practise - of how training is or is 
not dealt with has been addressed here before, Mr Dashwood.  The thought 
that people might be trained or taught seems to have perished at many 
places I've seen... and I recall one or two others here who had similar 
observations.

>
>Even if I never used it, I'd like to think the tool was available, in case I 
>DID need it.

'DID' (caps original) need, Mr Dashwood... or were clever enough to 
manufacture a need?  I know of a shop where the manager proved he DID need 
a hardware upgrade... by loading down the present machine with running 
unnecessary and redundant copies of PROFS.  Fiendishly clever, these 
human-being type folks, eh?

DD

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ALTER design (Was: Code problems with Perform Thru Exit causes fall through)

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2007-08-01T09:21:24-06:00

On Wed, 1 Aug 2007 13:28:33 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>I'm not suggesting we should bring back ALTER (although I wouldn't mind if 
>it was re-instated), I think I am just mourning the fact that programming 
>gets "dumbed down" to the lowest common denominator because some people are 
>not capable of handling power tools.

That's why automatic transmissions out-sell stick shifts.

But we are moving simultaneously to a "dumbed down" world and a much
more complex world.    It's harder to use a phone or a TV than it used
to be.    

OO programming is an example of one tool that is simultaneously more
simple and more complex.   We need to know less and we need to know
more at the same time.    The simplifications are valuable as our
domain grows.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5hd012F3k2a0vU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<eWrqi.35$IE5.12@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net> <qKvqi.105614$wG2.60480@newsfe17.lga> <1185584321.018963.77560@e9g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <13alet62ejv7f91@corp.supernews.com> <1185663930.742550.293780@z24g2000prh.googlegroups.com> <13ao1utdpul1cc@corp.supernews.com> <5h3djvF3imk7tU1@mid.individual.net> <3i3sa3hcmfal9nepkknnke9cq2eu4981c5@4ax.com> <5h78qqF3hguocU1@mid.individual.net> <anhua3179feekktorvlr509prhm4vt9oji@4ax.com> <13b1bnopga84f23@news.supernews.com> <3fh1b35ker8kbfv4esmke6i9ifmcrops4e@4ax.com>`

```


"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:3fh1b35ker8kbfv4esmke6i9ifmcrops4e@4ax.com...
> On Wed, 1 Aug 2007 11:04:18 -0500, "HeyBub" <heybubNOSPAM@gmail.com>
> wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Different types of arcana.

No, my experience is that there is less arcana, and more explanation and 
example.

Given the true meaning of "arcana", there is much in COBOL that is hidden. 
It isn't "secret" it just isn't available openly, because the user base (in 
Internet terms) is "small". It isn't worth writing articles about it and 
explaining it because the ROI on doing so is minimal.

Also, much of what is pertinent to COBOL, simply isn't pertinent to the 
world outside of COBOL. Furthermore, as we have seen here recently, there is 
fierce division over COBOL styles and so there will be argument even if you 
DID try and explain some of the conceptual stuff in simple, easily 
assimilable language. (It is a couple of years now since I wrote anything 
publicly on COBOL; it just isn't worth the effort...)

Let me try and explain with an example from what I am actually doing at the 
moment.

I couldn't understand why, having created a cookie and set the expiry date, 
if I later tested that expiry, it was inexplicably reset to the mininum 
DateTime. I traced it in VS 2005 and could see my compare failing every 
time, and the Expires property of the cookie showing the minimum date. (even 
though it showed the correct expiry date when I created it...)

Instead of consulting ASP.NET help I simply GOOGLEd... [C# ASP.NET cookie 
expiry] Immediately, an article describing in detail exactly what I was 
trying to do, comprehensive C# example code, and in 20 minutes I was back on 
the road, with my cookies now working correctly. (The problem was in my 
understanding of the word "Expires" and how I expected it to behave...)

With this kind of "Help" at your fingertips, you don't need to visit forums 
for support very often, and when you do it is usually because you are 
pioneering something, or want to compare notes with others who've done what 
you did, or need more general background, or something simply isn't working 
the way it is supposed to. Many MicroSoft people frequent these forums and 
they are happy to help. Some of the information is remarkable. They also 
post on what is going on and offer sample code. I received assistance from 
one such person for what was not even a MS problem (it was a problem with 
the Fujitsu NetCOBOL Run Time optimization). He didn't solve my problem for 
me, but he shed insight into it and pointed me in the right direction so I 
could solve it. And I did, and learned much in the process... :-)

So the nature of the forums is different from what we have here. The average 
age of the people who frequent the forums is lower than we have here too, 
and that means a different mindset... :-)

Pete.
```

"HeyBub" <heybubNOSPAM@gmail.com> wrote in message 
news:13b1bnopga84f23@news.supernews.com...
> Howard Brazee wrote:
>> On Tue, 31 Jul 2007 10:59:38 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> see here?

I am spending a bit of time in the C# forums. I can report that, as far as I 
can see so far, they don't. :-)

I think they are comprised of younger people who are much more concerned 
with getting solutions than arguing about style, or reminiscing on the 
"Glory Days" (they aren't old enough to have had any, for the most part 
:-)), or arguing for the sake of rhetoric (many can't spell or speak English 
very well and it isn't their first language, so they use it only for the 
exchange of information, not for fun...), and the nature of the programming 
is such that there really isn't that much variation in style anyway. Because 
code is more succinct and less verbose, you can look at it and see 
immediately whether it is useful or not. That is all that seems to really 
matter.

There is a different atmosphere in the C# forums. I made no secret of being 
a "COBOL guy" and only encountered one hostile reaction which was quickly 
jumped on by others. For the most part, I was made welcome and received 
help. I look forward to being able to reciprocate when I have acquired 
enough expertise. I already have been able to help in some very specific 
areas like COM Interop with C#, but I can see that my level of expertise is 
growing. I look at code I wrote 3 months ago and quickly change it to be 
more efficient. The full richness of the component paradigm is starting to 
really sink in and I am very happy and comfortable in the C# space.

Having said that, it is more fun to post here... :-) (Well, most of the 
time...:-))


>
> I swear sometimes I feel like starting a thread on the untimely demise of 
> the NOTE paragraph.
>

I think you definitely should :-)

And then we could go on for days about the subtle difference between using 
it at the start of a paragraph and using it on a line...

Pete.
```

> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>>

Because NOTE is a reserved word?

I have been known (when I was a young and callow youth) to code stuff like:

COMMENTS SECTION.
CS001.
        NOTE THAT BLAH...BLAH...BLAH    YADA YADA YADA...
        GENERAL COMMENTS ABOUT ANYTHING, THAT MIGHT BE USEFUL TO MAINTENANCE 
PROGRAMMER
        ...

CS999.
        EXIT.

MAIN SECTION.
MS001.
        PERFORM INITIALIZATION.
        PERFORM MAINLINE UNTIL FINISHED.
        PERFORM CLOSE-DOWN.
        STOP RUN.
MS999.
        EXIT.

INITIALIZATION SECTION.
INIT001.
         ....
etc.

(No-one ever complained about this SECTIONed code or asked me to stop doing 
it...:-))

Another lifetime... happy days!

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code problems with Perform Thru Exit causes fall through

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** spambait@milmac.com (Doug Miller)
- **Date:** 2007-08-01T01:38:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<JtRri.479$IE5.467@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net>`
- **References:** `<1185223084.744466.291540@22g2000hsm.googlegroups.com> <1185565019.612580.222200@e9g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <eWrqi.35$IE5.12@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net> <qKvqi.105614$wG2.60480@newsfe17.lga> <1185584321.018963.77560@e9g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <13alet62ejv7f91@corp.supernews.com> <eg2sa3dfhomcm0nv9jd37o4h7ghq6eet45@4ax.com> <13asanp7chflce6@corp.supernews.com> <fefsa3tko050t39dj8t5r8e2olq6c2oalp@4ax.com> <13asqtvhafjldf5@corp.supernews.com> <IeFri.46485$YL5.42322@newssvr29.news.prodigy.net> <13autmji2v05a09@corp.supernews.com> <%JLri.1722$ox5.147@nlpi068.nbdc.sbc.com> <13avknk1fk9e1a0@corp.supernews.com>`

```
In article <13avknk1fk9e1a0@corp.supernews.com>, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:

>Sections are no more "*inherently* drop-through" than
>paragraphs. 

That is incorrect.

>Performing a section (or paragraph) causes
>a transfer of control to the first statement. What happens
>after that depends entirely on the statements that are
>executed. 

Performing a paragraph causes a transfer of control to the first statement in 
the paragraph. Absent any further transfers of control, at the end of that 
paragraph, control returns to the statement following the PERFORM.

Performing a section causes a transfer of control to the first statement in 
the first paragraph of the section. Absent any further transfers on control, 
at the end of that paragraph, control drops through to the first statement of 
the subsequent paragraph.

>Specifically, there is no rule regarding sections
>that requires each paragraph in a section to be executed
>sequentially (The exceptions are irrelevant because the
>premise is false.). 

The difference lies in the default behavior. In a section, the default 
behavior is to drop through.

>The rule is that statements be executed
>sequentially and that rule applies to paragraphs and
>sections, equally.

Incorrect, for reasons already described.
>
>
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code problems with Perform Thru Exit causes fall through

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2007-08-01T00:44:37-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13b043eg9h0ql4d@corp.supernews.com>`

```

"Doug Miller" <spambait@milmac.com> wrote in message
news:JtRri.479$IE5.467@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net...
> In article <13avknk1fk9e1a0@corp.supernews.com>, "Rick Smith"
<ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
>
> >Sections are no more "*inherently* drop-through" than
> >paragraphs.
>
> That is incorrect.

[snip]

> >Specifically, there is no rule regarding sections
> >that requires each paragraph in a section to be executed
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> behavior is to drop through.

Because the standard does not, in regard to flow control,
distinguish between paragraphs in sections, and not; the
default (or "inherent") behavior of paragraphs is to
"drop-through". Something you claimed, above, was
incorrect.

What you seem to have done, is to attribute to paragraphs
the behavior of perform. That would be an error of commission.

> >The rule is that statements be executed
> >sequentially and that rule applies to paragraphs and
> >sections, equally.
>
> Incorrect, for reasons already described.

I paraphrased the standard (14.5.3) rather than quoting it.
So, according to you the standard is incorrect.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code problems with Perform Thru Exit causes fall through

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2007-08-01T03:08:30-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13b0cfmlcf39825@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<1185223084.744466.291540@22g2000hsm.googlegroups.com> <1185565019.612580.222200@e9g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <eWrqi.35$IE5.12@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net> <qKvqi.105614$wG2.60480@newsfe17.lga> <1185584321.018963.77560@e9g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <13alet62ejv7f91@corp.supernews.com> <eg2sa3dfhomcm0nv9jd37o4h7ghq6eet45@4ax.com> <13asanp7chflce6@corp.supernews.com> <fefsa3tko050t39dj8t5r8e2olq6c2oalp@4ax.com> <13asqtvhafjldf5@corp.supernews.com> <IeFri.46485$YL5.42322@newssvr29.news.prodigy.net> <13autmji2v05a09@corp.supernews.com> <%JLri.1722$ox5.147@nlpi068.nbdc.sbc.com> <13avknk1fk9e1a0@corp.supernews.com> <JtRri.479$IE5.467@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net> <13b043eg9h0ql4d@corp.supernews.com> <1185945332.451259.320410@m37g2000prh.googlegroups.com>`

```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:1185945332.451259.320410@m37g2000prh.googlegroups.com...
[snip]
> You seem to think that 'default' means having an 'EXIT SECTION' in the
> first paragraph while what it actually means is 'without explicit
> control statements', thus you fail to see the argument being made by
> Doug.

No, I know that "default", in the context, means "failure
to act". While one may override the default behavior by
a PERFORM statement or avoid the default behavior by
explicit control statements; that does not, in any way,
change the default behavior. Thus, Mr Miller's statement,
"In a section, the default behavior is to drop through." is
incorrect. "[T]o drop through" is the default behavior of
paragraphs wherever they occur in a COBOL program.

I suppose what Mr Miller should have said is that
performing single paragraphs only guarantees that no
paragraph will exhibit its default behavior by dropping
through to the following paragraph. This is a strong and
sensible reason for using paragraphs only. Instead, he
took a negative approach by trying to attack sections
and those who use them ("[they] should be shot").
Unfortunately, his arguments were so full of errors, both
of commission and of omission, that I find it impossible
to see his argument against sections.
```

> "Richard" <rip...@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> paragraphs wherever they occur in a COBOL program.

Then the problem is that you are using the term 'drop through' as if
it could be used to mean from statement to statement while the common
usage is from paragraph to paragraph.

Merely executing one statement after the previous one is _not_ 'drop
through', 'through' implying 'through the label'.


> I suppose what Mr Miller should have said is that
> performing single paragraphs only guarantees that no
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code problems with Perform Thru Exit causes fall through

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2007-08-01T12:51:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f8pvkn$dlc$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<1185223084.744466.291540@22g2000hsm.googlegroups.com> <13autmji2v05a09@corp.supernews.com> <%JLri.1722$ox5.147@nlpi068.nbdc.sbc.com> <jKSdndgp06PBeTLbnZ2dnUVZ_qWtnZ2d@comcast.com>`

```
In article <jKSdndgp06PBeTLbnZ2dnUVZ_qWtnZ2d@comcast.com>,
LX-i  <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

[snip]

>Who is the arbiter of "good" COBOL, or whether a coding style is a 
>"problem" or not?

The person who signs the checks for the project, of course.  In my 
experience this person is usually satisfied if:

1) All paragraphs are performed THRU a matching -EXIT with no intervening 
paragraph names between PARA-NAME and PARA-NAME-EXIT.

2) GO TO is allowed to refer only to the -EXIT paragraph immediately 
following, the PARA-NAME immediately preceding or the -ABEND routine for 
the program... in some instances LEAVE-THIS-PROGRAM (in subroutines) may 
be substituted for -ABEND.

>If someone's code does what it is supposed to do, and 
>they document it well, I really don't see the problem.

If the person who signs the check for a project is not smiling then there 
is a problem.

[snip]

>100-PROCESS-EQPP.
>     FETCH NEXT EQPP-158 WITHIN UNIT-TO-ACFT SET.
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>Now, I don't particularly care for this style - but is it bad COBOL?

If ERROR-HANDLER is the -ABEND or the LEAVE-THIS-PROGRAM paragraph then it 
violates neither 1) nor 2) given above, therefore it would, in my 
experience, make most signers-of-checks smile.

[snip]

>It could be written 
>differently...
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>I don't like it.

There is no -EXIT paragraph shown; this violates 1) above.  If 
ERROR-HANDLER is not (see above) then this violates 2).  Other folks might 
not smile, as well.

[snip]

>100-PROCESS-EQUIP.
>     FETCH FIRST EQPP-158 WITHIN UNIT-TO-EQPP SET.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>     EXIT.

Neither paragraph can be performed through its matching -EXIT, violating 
1).  GO TO range is outside of an immediately preceding paragraph or the 
matching -EXIT.  Someone who signs checks will, most likely, not be 
smiling.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code problems with Perform Thru Exit causes fall through

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2007-08-01T09:28:22-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vf91b35avgfvk393md3tj1fe3p4h2u9v1d@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<qKvqi.105614$wG2.60480@newsfe17.lga> <1185584321.018963.77560@e9g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <13alet62ejv7f91@corp.supernews.com> <eg2sa3dfhomcm0nv9jd37o4h7ghq6eet45@4ax.com> <13asanp7chflce6@corp.supernews.com> <fefsa3tko050t39dj8t5r8e2olq6c2oalp@4ax.com> <13asqtvhafjldf5@corp.supernews.com> <IeFri.46485$YL5.42322@newssvr29.news.prodigy.net> <13autmji2v05a09@corp.supernews.com> <%JLri.1722$ox5.147@nlpi068.nbdc.sbc.com> <jKSdndgp06PBeTLbnZ2dnUVZ_qWtnZ2d@comcast.com>`

```
On Tue, 31 Jul 2007 19:39:34 -0600, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

>Who is the arbiter of "good" COBOL, or whether a coding style is a 
>"problem" or not?  If someone's code does what it is supposed to do, and 
>they document it well, I really don't see the problem.

CoBOL is supposed to be self-documenting, and I do see that
maintenance programmers trust the code much more than the
documentation in my experience.

The useful part of documentation is saying "why", not "what".

If there is no maintenance, then documentation isn't needed, and
neither are programming styles designed to make mistake-free
maintenance easier.

But the CoBOL community isn't about one-time code.   We expect our
code to be maintained.   Our coding style should be designed to
optimize this process.
```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:vf91b35avgfvk393md3tj1fe3p4h2u9v1d@4ax.com...
> On Tue, 31 Jul 2007 19:39:34 -0600, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> optimize this process.

Insightful post Howard.

It does raise some questions, though...

1.  Do we need to use solutions that require constant maintenance at a line 
by line level?
2. Can we afford to do so?
3. Are there "better" alternatives?

More and more corporations are having to ask these questions and the answers 
are not favourable to a future for COBOL.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code problems with Perform Thru Exit causes fall through

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2007-08-02T11:51:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f8sgft$7di$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<qKvqi.105614$wG2.60480@newsfe17.lga> <jKSdndgp06PBeTLbnZ2dnUVZ_qWtnZ2d@comcast.com> <vf91b35avgfvk393md3tj1fe3p4h2u9v1d@4ax.com> <5hd0mkF3jjum5U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
In article <5hd0mkF3jjum5U1@mid.individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
>
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>2. Can we afford to do so?

Both of these statements appear to be about an amporphous 'we'... I wonder 
how that is defined.  While programmers might be, at times, consulted with 
about solutions most do not, to the best of my knowledge, have the 
authority to sign off on any but the most trivial of expenses.

>3. Are there "better" alternatives?

I hope that the quotation-marks around 'better' were intended to scream 
loudly; to me they do, perhaps I am sensitive.

What is 'better'... taking time up-front to do something right or taking 
time later to do something over?  The answer is obvious to me... and 
equally obvious, and opposite, to the managers in charge of development 
and maintenance; maintenance costs don't come out of the development 
budget so shoddy work up front means congratulations at a department-level 
status meeting for the one... and another in an ever-growing pile of 
fettucine for the other.

>
>More and more corporations are having to ask these questions and the answers 
>are not favourable to a future for COBOL.

Such questions are not new, Mr Dashwood... the tools available for dealing 
with them might change but that's about all.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code problems with Perform Thru Exit causes fall through

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2007-08-02T08:21:59-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jmp3b39ilro60nuudjh2pfco938mojbgq9@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<13alet62ejv7f91@corp.supernews.com> <eg2sa3dfhomcm0nv9jd37o4h7ghq6eet45@4ax.com> <13asanp7chflce6@corp.supernews.com> <fefsa3tko050t39dj8t5r8e2olq6c2oalp@4ax.com> <13asqtvhafjldf5@corp.supernews.com> <IeFri.46485$YL5.42322@newssvr29.news.prodigy.net> <13autmji2v05a09@corp.supernews.com> <%JLri.1722$ox5.147@nlpi068.nbdc.sbc.com> <jKSdndgp06PBeTLbnZ2dnUVZ_qWtnZ2d@comcast.com> <vf91b35avgfvk393md3tj1fe3p4h2u9v1d@4ax.com> <5hd0mkF3jjum5U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Thu, 2 Aug 2007 15:17:35 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>It does raise some questions, though...
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>are not favourable to a future for COBOL.

Over time, the size of our components increases.   The electronics
technician replaces boards, not tubes.    If we repair our TV at all,
it's at this level - but more likely we throw it out.

Sure there are some people designing the transistors and assembly code
- but mostly we use components that we trust.   And some people still
cut their own two-by-fours to build a house, while others buy their
walls separately.

With CoBOL, our trustworthy components are pretty much at a line
number - old-timers who checked their assembly code are endangered.

If we create with larger components, we maintain with larger
components (or have disposable applications).     I expect this trend
to continue in the future.
```

If we design and document correctly then it might not be necessary to
amend because we could enhance the design/documentation and re-code

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code problems with Perform Thru Exit causes fall through

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-08-01T15:41:56+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5hado6F3i25u4U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1185223084.744466.291540@22g2000hsm.googlegroups.com> <1185565019.612580.222200@e9g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <eWrqi.35$IE5.12@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net> <qKvqi.105614$wG2.60480@newsfe17.lga> <1185584321.018963.77560@e9g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <13alet62ejv7f91@corp.supernews.com> <eg2sa3dfhomcm0nv9jd37o4h7ghq6eet45@4ax.com> <13asanp7chflce6@corp.supernews.com> <1185835364.404470.56690@m37g2000prh.googlegroups.com> <13at87dgirq9jcc@corp.supernews.com> <1185865451.293160.20430@j4g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <13avhio8o8klo93@corp.supernews.com> <kWPri.471$IE5.5@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net> <13avli6dnoucoa3@corp.supernews.com> <_uRri.480$IE5.221@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net>`

```


"Doug Miller" <spambait@milmac.com> wrote in message 
news:_uRri.480$IE5.221@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net...
> In article <13avli6dnoucoa3@corp.supernews.com>, "Rick Smith" 
> <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Nope. Rick's right. It IS DISTINCT (and whether or not it is self-contained 
depends on the way the person writing the program has coded it).

You are also right that any paragraph within the SECTION can be PERFORMED 
from anywhere, but that is part of what is leading to the debate here.

If you consider a section to be a paragraph, then your comments and position 
are perfectly correct.

The point is, that many of us who use SECTIONs, DON'T see them as we would a 
paragraph. (this point was noted by Rick some days ago).

That means we DON'T perform paragraphs within them, from outside of them 
(or, in my case, even inside of them. I ONLY PERFORM a SECTION (or an inline 
perform, of course), or expect control to 'drop through', or any of the 
myriad other poor practices that apply to control in COBOL  programming.

You need to change your current perspective if you really want to understand 
why people use SECTIONed code. (But I suspect you don't ... your mind was 
made up long ago :-) So are the minds of most of the participants here (self 
included...); you don't seriously think for one minute that a bitter debate 
in CLC is going to change the way people code...?)

All of the pitfalls you are aware of are absolutely true if one considers a 
SECTION to be a paragraph (amongst other paragraphs)..

If one doesn't, then there are actually some benefits available from 
SECTIONed code.

One that hasn't been mentioned is that it is compliant with the COBOL 
hierarchy (Division>Section>Paragraph>Sentence>Statement) so it makes 
consistent logical sense to group code for local functions into Sections. I 
agree, that's a trivial one.

If you write it without paragraphs in it  (apart from the mandatory one at 
the start), then most of the objections you have evaporate.
You CAN'T use GO TOs or PERFORMs from inside or outside of it, so that looks 
like a step in the right direction. But, hang on a minute, then what you 
have a is just a big paragraph so why use a SECTION?

Well, why not? :-) It makes no difference, so why fight about it? It is 
simply a matter of what you prefer. Style. Pure and simple.

I use SECTIONs when coding COBOL much the same way as I use an outline view 
when writing an article or paper. It gives me a framework to hang the big 
ideas on and get them organised before attending to the nitty-gritty. I 
usually code the section headers as I identify the function which that 
particular section is going to be responsible for, then I put EXIT stubs in 
them so I can test the flow of control as it develops. I'd be the first to 
admit that this may be rooted in habits developed before such exotic 
constructs as EXIT SECTION  or EXIT PARAGRAPH were even thought of. 
Nevertheless, this approach works extremely well for me (and has done for 
nearly 40 years) but I am under no illusions about it being the ONLY way, or 
even the best way. (and I wouldn't consider shooting people who have other 
ideas :-))

As mentioned elsewhere, I'll fix whatever's broken, no matter how it's 
coded. And you won't hear me whingeing about it :-) (I might charge you more 
if what you give me is truly apalling :-))

Like most people, I code what I am COMFORTABLE with coding. (And compliant 
with shop standards, if it is code for someone else, that I have no control 
over.)

For you it's paragraphs, for me it's sections. You say "tomayto", I say 
"tomarto", it still doesn't change the flavour of the Bolognese... :-)

Pete.
```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:1185944048.572319.273640@x40g2000prg.googlegroups.com...
> On Aug 1, 3:41 pm, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[105 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Hey! No fair! I only use them as stubs until that section gets actually 
written :-)  I USED to always have an exit at the end of the section, but I 
realised (all by myself) that unless it was referenced, it was redundant. As 
my confidence with GOTOLess programming increased, and my reluctance to 
reference an exit (unless there was a REAL need to get out immediately, in 
something like a time-sensitive process control application (and we don't 
get to write a lot of those in COBOL)), I just stopped coding them.

Now, having achieved that major breakthrough, you don't seriously expect me 
to give up SECTION all together do you :-)?  I like it. Find it COMFORTABLE.

Probably just as well I'm quitting COBOL, I obviously can't handle it.. ;-)

(A lifetime... wasted... oh, the pain... <exit stage left, sobbing...>:-))

Pete
```

<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>That means we DON'T perform paragraphs within them, from outside of them 
>(or, in my case, even inside of them. I ONLY PERFORM a SECTION (or an inline 
>perform, of course), or expect control to 'drop through', or any of the 
>myriad other poor practices that apply to control in COBOL  programming.

But others have noted, that a maintenance programmer can inadvertently
perform paragraphs from within them, from outside of them.   What
style someone can use for effective working code may or may not be the
optimal style for good effective maintenance of such code).

(I do to - on purpose.  I perform paragraphs in my REST-OF-THE-PROGRAM
SECTION.).

> But, hang on a minute, then what you 
>have a is just a big paragraph so why use a SECTION?
>
>Well, why not? :-) It makes no difference, so why fight about it? It is 
>simply a matter of what you prefer. Style. Pure and simple.

Why not?  To slightly reduce the chance that someone's maintenance of
your program will get you a call in the middle of the night.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code problems with Perform Thru Exit causes fall through

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-08-01T01:26:06-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1185956766.134306.288780@m37g2000prh.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<13b06e13pc6ttf1@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<1185223084.744466.291540@22g2000hsm.googlegroups.com> <1185565019.612580.222200@e9g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <eWrqi.35$IE5.12@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net> <qKvqi.105614$wG2.60480@newsfe17.lga> <1185584321.018963.77560@e9g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <13alet62ejv7f91@corp.supernews.com> <eg2sa3dfhomcm0nv9jd37o4h7ghq6eet45@4ax.com> <13asanp7chflce6@corp.supernews.com> <1185835364.404470.56690@m37g2000prh.googlegroups.com> <13at87dgirq9jcc@corp.supernews.com> <1185865451.293160.20430@j4g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <13avhio8o8klo93@corp.supernews.com> <1185936508.704823.215900@j4g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <13b06e13pc6ttf1@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Aug 1, 5:25 pm, "Rick Smith" <ricksm...@mfi.net> wrote:
> "Richard" <rip...@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message

> > > > No. SECTIONs may be the basis of segmentation. Or they may be the
> > > > basis of a hierarchical program structure with a sub-structure of
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> Not at all.

Are you denying that you clipped it off or that it was deliberate ?

> The two elements that had to be shown were
> "requires" and "entity". I included the ellipsis to show that
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> necessary!

No. Wrong. You clipped it so that you didn't have to argue against
'any more so than ..', or 'with special privileges and attributes'.

First of all there is actually no demonstrated 'requirement' that the
compiler treat a section as an 'entity' at all. It certainly will
treat a group of sections with the same priority as being an 'entity'
when it is segmenting - it will generate different object blocks. It
may well change name spaces when it encounters a section header, but
otherwise it may be outputting just code.

Secondly the issue was that you were attempting to give some special
attributes and features to the SECTION word which you think recreated
the source as a 'mini=program', 'encapsulated' and 'solid line' when
this had nothing to do with it having the 'section' word and
everything to do with how you designed and wrote the program and could
be much the same whether it used the section word or not.

> > > [entity: being or existence, esp. when considered
> > > as distinct or self-contained] A COBOL compiler will
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> it will.

I denied that it would treat a section as an 'entity' that would earn
it a 'solid line'. In fact I will challenge you to examine the output
executable code from a compiler and identify where each section starts
and ends. If the compiler is treating it as 'distinct' then this
should be obvious.

I added the 'it will recognise each paragraph as something
distinct ...' in the hope that it would show you that your claim over
'entity' was trivial.

Whether a section is 'self-contained' in some way is not inherent in
it having a section keyword but is a feature of code designed to be
that whether it be a section or not.

But it certainly does not match my idea of 'self-contained' because
the data areas are not within it, as they can be with, say, sub-
programs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code problems with Perform Thru Exit causes fall through

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2007-08-01T13:27:30-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13b1goeuhk2c12@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<1185223084.744466.291540@22g2000hsm.googlegroups.com> <1185565019.612580.222200@e9g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <eWrqi.35$IE5.12@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net> <qKvqi.105614$wG2.60480@newsfe17.lga> <1185584321.018963.77560@e9g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <13alet62ejv7f91@corp.supernews.com> <eg2sa3dfhomcm0nv9jd37o4h7ghq6eet45@4ax.com> <13asanp7chflce6@corp.supernews.com> <1185835364.404470.56690@m37g2000prh.googlegroups.com> <13at87dgirq9jcc@corp.supernews.com> <1185865451.293160.20430@j4g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <13avhio8o8klo93@corp.supernews.com> <1185936508.704823.215900@j4g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <13b06e13pc6ttf1@corp.supernews.com> <1185956766.134306.288780@m37g2000prh.googlegroups.com>`

```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:1185956766.134306.288780@m37g2000prh.googlegroups.com...
> On Aug 1, 5:25 pm, "Rick Smith" <ricksm...@mfi.net> wrote:
> > "Richard" <rip...@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > > > paragraphs. Neither requires they be treated as an 'entity' any
more
> > > > > so than a paragraph is or a statement is. _You_ may be treating
them
> > > > > as an 'entity' with special privileges and attributes but the
compiler
> > > > > does not.
> >
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> Are you denying that you clipped it off or that it was deliberate ?

Denial of the former makes the latter irrelevant. I will deny
the former because, as I explained, the omitted words were
replaced by an ellipsis and, therefore, not "clipped off".

I truly do not understand why you are raising this issue.
The omitted words, in the original constext, suggest that
you had changed your position from absolute denial to
qualified acceptance of the assertion "the compiler will
treat each section as an entity"; however, the comparative
"any more so than", it seems to me, is still somewhat vague.

> > The two elements that had to be shown were
> > "requires" and "entity". I included the ellipsis to show that
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 'any more so than ..', or 'with special privileges and attributes'.

First, I did not clip it.

Second, there was no point in my arguing against "any more so
than ..." since it seems to agree with my assertion.

Third, I have no idea what you mean by "with special privileges
and attributes", which, it seems to me, may be an exaggeration
of my actual claims.

> First of all there is actually no demonstrated 'requirement' that the
> compiler treat a section as an 'entity' at all. It certainly will
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> be much the same whether it used the section word or not.

Not "special attributes and features", just useful ones
that are available only when using sections.

> > > > [entity: being or existence, esp. when considered
> > > > as distinct or self-contained] A COBOL compiler will
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> should be obvious.

Well that's a problem! You are treating "solid line" in absolute
terms. I am treating "solid line" versus "dashed line" in relative
terms as the comparison of sections versus paragraph groups.

> I added the 'it will recognise each paragraph as something
> distinct ...' in the hope that it would show you that your claim over
> 'entity' was trivial.

Then you seem close to realizing that the compiler will recognize
each section and each paragraph as things distinct; but will not
recognize any paragraph group, at all. Given that, which of the
two: section or paragraph group, is more deserving of the "solid
line" and which of the "dashed line"?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code problems with Perform Thru Exit causes fall through

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-08-01T13:18:00-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1185999480.575306.65110@q3g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<13b1goeuhk2c12@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<1185223084.744466.291540@22g2000hsm.googlegroups.com> <1185565019.612580.222200@e9g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <eWrqi.35$IE5.12@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net> <qKvqi.105614$wG2.60480@newsfe17.lga> <1185584321.018963.77560@e9g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <13alet62ejv7f91@corp.supernews.com> <eg2sa3dfhomcm0nv9jd37o4h7ghq6eet45@4ax.com> <13asanp7chflce6@corp.supernews.com> <1185835364.404470.56690@m37g2000prh.googlegroups.com> <13at87dgirq9jcc@corp.supernews.com> <1185865451.293160.20430@j4g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <13avhio8o8klo93@corp.supernews.com> <1185936508.704823.215900@j4g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <13b06e13pc6ttf1@corp.supernews.com> <1185956766.134306.288780@m37g2000prh.googlegroups.com> <13b1goeuhk2c12@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Aug 2, 5:27 am, "Rick Smith" <ricksm...@mfi.net> wrote:
> "Richard" <rip...@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
>
…[109 more quoted lines elided]…
> > should be obvious.
On Aug 2, 5:27 am, "Rick Smith" <ricksm...@mfi.net> wrote:
> "Richard" <rip...@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message

> > > > You deliberately clipped off the: "any more so than a paragraph or
> > > > statement is.".
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Denial of the former makes the latter irrelevant.

You had already pointed out that 'or' only requires one to be true.

> I will deny
> the former because, as I explained, the omitted words were
> replaced by an ellipsis and, therefore, not "clipped off".

You seem to invent new, more convenient, meanings foe words and
phrases. If the words have been ommitted they are 'clipped off'.

> I truly do not understand why you are raising this issue.
> The omitted words, in the original constext, suggest that
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> "any more so than", it seems to me, is still somewhat vague.

You were using 'entity' to attempt to support your claim about
sections giving some properties of 'encapsulation' as if it made it
like a sub-program. But you never even attempeted to explain why any
'treating it like an entity', if it does, did anything useful that
matched your claims.

> > No. Wrong. You clipped it so that you didn't have to argue against
> > 'any more so than ..', or 'with special privileges and attributes'.
>
> First, I did not clip it.

Well I looked at the quote you argued against and the words weren't
there. Your argument ignored those words.

> Second, there was no point in my arguing against "any more so
> than ..." since it seems to agree with my assertion.

You were supporting claims about sections by asserting that the
compiler treated them as an 'entity' as if this was something
different. If the compiler treats paragraphs, statements, words,
literals, full stops, divisions, and blank spaces as 'entities' then
your is a meaningless claim (as they all seem to be). It is a
distinction with no difference.

> Third, I have no idea what you mean by "with special privileges
> and attributes", which, it seems to me, may be an exaggeration
> of my actual claims.

Your claims were that the _compiler_ makes SECTIONs (with the keyword)
have the attributes of 'encapsulation', 'self-contained' and
'distinct'. None of which you have supported except by vague claims
that it is an 'entity' without explaining how this translates into
your claims.

Now I grant that your code may be contructed such that various parts
of the code do have those attributes, at least in concept, but this is
because of the way the code is implemented, not because it has a
keyword attached to it.

That is there is nothing in the compiler that enforces any of those
attributes. I can write SECTIONs that are _not_ 'encapsulated',
'distinct' or 'self-contained', therefore the compiler treating them
as an 'entity' (if it does) does not mean anything.

> Not "special attributes and features", just useful ones
> that are available only when using sections.

There are two things that a section (ignoring segmentation which is
the actual purpose) gives:

1) some resolving of duplicate paragraph names.
2) ability to use non-recursive GO TO within a perform scope (or
equivalent with EXIT ~).

Neither are what I would call useful, neither are unique to sections.
Neither support your claim of 'encapsulation' or 'self-contained',
neither enforce an interface. Neither justify a 'solid line'.

In fact you said that you didn't need to use GO TO: """without exit
paragraphs or GO TO statements."""

> Well that's a problem! You are treating "solid line" in absolute
> terms. I am treating "solid line" versus "dashed line" in relative
> terms as the comparison of sections versus paragraph groups.

No. It is _you_ that is treating 'solid line' as something other than
your imaginings. You were attempting to illustrate 'encapsulation' and
'self-contained' as if the SECTION keyword made this happen.

> Then you seem close to realizing that the compiler will recognize
> each section and each paragraph as things distinct;

And each word, each full-stop, and each statement.

> but will not
> recognize any paragraph group, at all. Given that, which of the
> two: section or paragraph group, is more deserving of the "solid
> line" and which of the "dashed line"?

Whether a particular piece of code deserves a 'solid line' a 'dotted
line' or 'skull and crossbones' is a result of how well it is designed
and coded and not whether it has the SECTION keyword. Neither sections
nor groups of paragraphs enforce nor assist with any of what you have
claimed.

_Neither_ deserve any more than a box made of question marks because
the compiler will not tell you where the problems are.

With sub-programs you can get some _actual_ encapsulation, they are an
'entity' where it makes a difference. You get some _actual_ self-
containment, even to the point of nesting. They deserve a dotted line
because they still can access GLOBAL item.

Apart from the trivial and error-prone non-detection of duplicate
names the only actual difference between a similarly coded SECTION and
group of paragraphs is that the former allows GO TO _different_
paragraph name within the scope of the perform.

Which is what I originally said.

Just to remind you about your erroneous claims, the "special
attributes" that you claimed for the SECTION keyword were:

"""Well, no, that is not the 'only' purpose! Another is
encapsulating mini-programs as parts of a larger program.
This is similar to nested programs; but without the call
overhead or the structuring requirements for contained
and common programs."""

You may get [some of] the _appearance_ of nested programs in the
source by arranging your code in a particular way but you get none of
the benefits of them regardless of whether it has a SECTION keyword or
not.
```

is presented as a box made of a solid line, and, when

dashed line." Anything beyond that is from your

    COPY PREV-STATEMENTS-SAME-POINT.

> > Second, there was no point in my arguing against "any more so
> > than ..." since it seems to agree with my assertion.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> distinction with no difference.

The diffeence, regarding the compiler, is its treatment
of sections versus paragraph groups.

> > Third, I have no idea what you mean by "with special privileges
> > and attributes", which, it seems to me, may be an exaggeration
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> your claims.

I made no such claims! Specifically, "encapsulation" was
in regard to "mini-programs" and not merely sections;
"self-contained" was part of the defintion of "entity" and I
denied any applicability of the former term; and "distinct"
was in regard to the compiler treating sections, not making
them have anything! Whatever attributes a section may
have are defined by COBOL and the extensions of an
implementor; I require nothing more than a conforming
compiler.

> Now I grant that your code may be contructed such that various parts
> of the code do have those attributes, at least in concept, but this is
> because of the way the code is implemented, not because it has a
> keyword attached to it.

I claimed nothing different.

> That is there is nothing in the compiler that enforces any of those
> attributes. I can write SECTIONs that are _not_ 'encapsulated',
> 'distinct' or 'self-contained', therefore the compiler treating them
> as an 'entity' (if it does) does not mean anything.

    COPY PREV-STATEMENTS-SAME-POINT.

> > Not "special attributes and features", just useful ones
> > that are available only when using sections.
>
> There are two things that a section (ignoring segmentation which is
> the actual purpose) gives:

Whether "segmentation ... is the actual purpose" for sections
is, at best, a dubious claim. It suggests that sections were
developed as the means to implement segmentation then were
used in other divisions. It is far more reasonable to accept that
sections were intended to break up divisions and that using
sections as the basis for segmentation was convenient. But,
at that time I was dealing with puberty, not recommendations
reagarding COBOL.

> 1) some resolving of duplicate paragraph names.
> 2) ability to use non-recursive GO TO within a perform scope (or
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> neither enforce an interface. Neither justify a 'solid line'.

I never claimed "encapsulation" nor "self-contained" in
regard to sections.

> In fact you said that you didn't need to use GO TO: """without exit
> paragraphs or GO TO statements."""

"It is EXIT SECTION that allow[s] this to be accomplished
without exit paragraphs or GO TO statements."

> > Well that's a problem! You are treating "solid line" in absolute
> > terms. I am treating "solid line" versus "dashed line" in relative
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 'self-contained' as if the SECTION keyword made this happen.

You are mistaken.

> > Then you seem close to realizing that the compiler will recognize
> > each section and each paragraph as things distinct;
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
> and common programs."""

Amended by: "Isolation of local or private data requires
a naming convention."

And please note that *I* never used the term "special
attributes".

> You may get [some of] the _appearance_ of nested programs in the
> source by arranging your code in a particular way but you get none of
> the benefits of them regardless of whether it has a SECTION keyword or
> not.

That I get none of the benefits is acceptable because
I also get none of the problems.
```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:1186085255.177114.263120@e16g2000pri.googlegroups.com...
> On Aug 3, 4:22 am, "Rick Smith" <ricksm...@mfi.net> wrote:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> So, you agree that 'encapsulating' doesn't actually happen.

I agree that a purpose of sections does not include
encapsulating [the source codes of] mini-programs;
but still hold that a purpose of sections may include
encapsulating [the functionalities of] mini-programs.

> > And please note that *I* never used the term "special
> > attributes".
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> So, you agree they are not 'similar to nested programs'.

"... whether it has a SECTION keyword or not." and
"... they are not ...."?

In the context for the original was: This [purpose for
sections] is similar to [using] nested programs [for the
same purposes]; .... Where "purpose for sections"
was implied, "using" was omitted, and "for the same
purposes" was loosely implied by "mini-programs".
That is, I never intended "[Sections] is similar to
nested programs; ...."

[It might also have been construed as "This [purpose
for sections] is similar to [the purpose for] nested
programs; ...." That construction would be denied by
"mini-programs".]

I hold that sections encapsulating [the functionalities of]
mini-programs as parts of a larger program are similar to
[using] nested programs [for the same purposes].
However, using nested programs together has certain
problems, which were mentioned, previously.

I accept that there are cases where required functionalities
can not be implemented in mini-programs and, in those
cases, there can be no similarity to nested programs.
```

- **References:** `<eWrqi.35$IE5.12@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net> <qKvqi.105614$wG2.60480@newsfe17.lga> <1185584321.018963.77560@e9g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <13alet62ejv7f91@corp.supernews.com> <eg2sa3dfhomcm0nv9jd37o4h7ghq6eet45@4ax.com> <13asanp7chflce6@corp.supernews.com> <1185835364.404470.56690@m37g2000prh.googlegroups.com> <13at87dgirq9jcc@corp.supernews.com> <1185865451.293160.20430@j4g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <13avhio8o8klo93@corp.supernews.com> <1185936508.704823.215900@j4g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <13b06e13pc6ttf1@corp.supernews.com> <1185956766.134306.288780@m37g2000prh.googlegroups.com> <13b1goeuhk2c12@corp.supernews.com> <1185999480.575306.65110@q3g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <13b41gfnmv2ciff@corp.supernews.com> <1186085255.177114.263120@e16g2000pri.googlegroups.com> <13b5926opditra8@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Aug 3, 3:40 pm, "Rick Smith" <ricksm...@mfi.net> wrote:
> "Richard" <rip...@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
>
…[51 more quoted lines elided]…
> [using] nested programs [for the same purposes].

Yet above you agreed that none of the befefits accrue: "That I get
none of the benefits is acceptable".

Sticking the keyword SECTION on a bunch of paragraphs is similar to
not sticking on the keyword except the keyword allows you to use GO TO
from one paragraph to another (which you don't) plus adds the bug-trap
of not reporting when the same name is used twice.

Anything else is pure puffery.

> However, using nested programs together has certain
> problems, which were mentioned, previously.

Which were, IIRC, the 'overhead' of keying in a few headings plus
alleged reduced performance.  Have you actually done a test between:

     MOVE stuff to interface
     PERFORM something
     MOVE interface to stuuf

and:

     CALL something USING stuff

where something is a nested program ?

When I tested this with an optimising compiler I found the difference
to be insignificant.

Not that I use nested programs, preferring the flexability and
reusability of separate programs and not caring about a few
microseconds.


> I accept that there are cases where required functionalities
> can not be implemented in mini-programs and, in those
> cases, there can be no similarity to nested programs.
```

> On Aug 3, 3:40 pm, "Rick Smith" <ricksm...@mfi.net> wrote:
> > "Richard" <rip...@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > > > source by arranging your code in a particular way but you get none
of
> > > > > the benefits of them regardless of whether it has a SECTION
keyword or
> > > > > not.
> >
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> none of the benefits is acceptable".

I see that as a logical conclusion:
    no nested programs = no benefits of
    no nested programs = no problems of

> Sticking the keyword SECTION on a bunch of paragraphs is similar to
> not sticking on the keyword except the keyword allows you to use GO TO
> from one paragraph to another (which you don't) plus adds the bug-trap
> of not reporting when the same name is used twice.

I do not design with GO TO in mind; but will use GO TO,
within the same section, when I suspect it will significantly
improve performance.

> Anything else is pure puffery.
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> where something is a nested program ?

I have not done timed peformance, yet. About nine years
ago, I did have a program that was entirely nested; that is,
each nested program had one unnamed paragraph. I did
that to take advantage of local variables. Performance was
noticibly slower so I ripped it all out. The program currently
uses single paragraph sections, with two exceptions, and the
few parameters that were passed before are now moved
before the perform. One of the exceptions is a five
paragraph mini-program I added several months ago. I am
satisfied with the overall performance of the program and
probably will not modify it for quite some time.

I suspect that I may, over the next few days, modifiy a
different program (a smaller one I don't really care about)
to get some timing data. That program was built with
multi-paragraph sections; but without the "well-defined
interfaces" of mini-programs.

> When I tested this with an optimising compiler I found the difference
> to be insignificant.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> > cases, there can be no similarity to nested programs.
```

On Aug 3, 7:38 pm, "Rick Smith" <ricksm...@mfi.net> wrote:
> "Richard" <rip...@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
>
…[67 more quoted lines elided]…
>     no nested programs = no problems of

So your "similar to [using] nested programs" without anything being
similar is ... well, just puffery really.


> > Sticking the keyword SECTION on a bunch of paragraphs is similar to
> > not sticking on the keyword except the keyword allows you to use GO TO
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> improve performance.

You seem obsessed with micro-seconds and seem prepared to sacrifice
maintainability, and development time, in persuit of them.

I haven't worried unduly about code performance in business
applications since I moved on from needing to have 3 users on a single
2.5MHz 8085 CPU under M/PM running Level II Cobol and there wasn't
enough RAM to use .GNTs so we only had .INTs (byte code - like Java
does).

Even then the time taken by disk access to the shared data made code
performance of trivial interest.


> > Anything else is pure puffery.
>
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > cases, there can be no similarity to nested programs.
