"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:1186156242.952830.232880@i38g2000prf.googlegroups.com...
> On Aug 3, 7:38 pm, "Rick Smith" <ricksm...@mfi.net> wrote:
> > "Richard" <rip...@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > > > > > You may get [some of] the _appearance_ of nested programs in
the
> > > > > > > source by arranging your code in a particular way but you get
none
> > of
> > > > > > > the benefits of them regardless of whether it has a SECTION
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
> similar is ... well, just puffery really.

[H'm, "just" versus "unjust" puffery? <g>]

Until the other day, I did not know what "puffery" was.
My dictionary defines it as: undue or exaggerated praise,
as in publishing.

"[S]imilar to" is a comparative. In this case, I intended it
to help the reader imagine what a mini-program might do;
so "similar to" is "not the same as" or, simply "not".
I could also have said "similar to using C source files";
but there are more differences to explain (how extern and
static data and functions relate to COBOL) and that would
be less helpful than using a COBOL referent.

Because I see and experiment with different ways for
using COBOL, the comments, often made about
performing single paragraphs only, seem, to me, to be
pufferies.

>
> > > Sticking the keyword SECTION on a bunch of paragraphs is similar to
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> maintainability, and development time, in persuit of them.

Needing without knowing how seems fruitless.
Being prepared to sacrifice, does not mean sacrifice.


> > > Anything else is pure puffery.
```

"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
news:13b5mvvh10i8se0@corp.supernews.com...
>
> "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
> news:1186116918.928634.279660@x40g2000prg.googlegroups.com...
[snip]
> > Which were, IIRC, the 'overhead' of keying in a few headings plus
> > alleged reduced performance.  Have you actually done a test between:
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> > to be insignificant.

Using Micro Focus COBOL 3.2.24, I ran much
simpler tests than I had previously planned; but the
results are more reflective of 'overhead'.

Data that was passed on the 'call' to the nested
program was moved to, then from, the mini-program
interface, except in the last test where only a result
was obtained. In that case, only the move from the
interface was done; but each 'program' also did an
internal move. The ratios given are nested program
to mini-program (NP:MP).

8-bytes (to and from) 11.5:1
80-bytes (to and from) 3:1
80-bytes (global), 2-byte (result) 200:1

I would not call any of these insignificant.
```

…[10 more quoted lines elided]…

If you want to have code that does nothing then certainly PERFORM is
the fastest way to do it, through the totally anal could unroll it the
speed it up, but I doubt that having a single move would rate the
grand title of "mini-program".

For real code all those times are insignificant regardless of the
ratio of 'doing nothing with a call':'doing nothing with a perform'.

<snipped Tweedledum and Tweedledee exchange reminiscent of  two elephants
fighting over a currant bun (the very small kind that has a scarcity of 
currants in it...)>


While I have no intention of getting involved in this, or taking sides, I
offer the following as a matter of record:

>> There are two things that a section (ignoring segmentation which is
>> the actual purpose) gives:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The COBOL 59 compiler (which was the first one I ever worked on, in 1967),
offered SECTIONs in the Procedure Division.

I believe I had passed puberty because I remember a strong hankering for the
lead punch girl:-)

This was long before segmentation was available and the compiler ran under
TOS (Tape Operating System, disk drives still being a phenomenon confined to
laboratories.)

Please feel free to resume your bizarre discussion now...:-)

<exits stage left, eating a currant bun...>

Pete.
```

On Aug 4, 3:02 pm, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> <snipped Tweedledum and Tweedledee exchange reminiscent of  two elephants
> fighting over a currant bun (the very small kind that has a scarcity of
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> lead punch girl:-)

Been there, done that, still have one here (though obviously not the
same one as yours).

> This was long before segmentation was available and the compiler ran under
> TOS (Tape Operating System, disk drives still being a phenomenon confined to
> laboratories.)

Actually my references have it that segmentation was in COBOL-61 and
it may have been earlier versions, but also may have been optional.

XE13 was a '61 compiler and certainly had segmentation on tape loaded
programs. ICL TP4118 "Introduction to COBOL", Jan 1969, is based on
Cobol'61 and JOD 65 has segementation


I recall one program that was badly coded  (not by me) with respect of
performing a SECTION and reloaded the overlay for each tape record.
The programmer had done something like:

     PERFORM Read-Tape UNTIL EOF

Where it was READ-TAPE SECTION 65.

"""This concept, originally implemented for assembly languages, was
very early incorporated in COBOL; the language features have changed
only in name in ANS 68 and ANS 74."""

"""Only two, very simple language features are invovled in
segmentation: the use of SECTION headers to partition the Procedure
Division, and the optional SEGMENT-LIMIT clause in the OBJECT-COMPUTER
paragraph."""
```

- **References:** `<13at87dgirq9jcc@corp.supernews.com> <1185865451.293160.20430@j4g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <13avhio8o8klo93@corp.supernews.com> <1185936508.704823.215900@j4g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <13b06e13pc6ttf1@corp.supernews.com> <1185956766.134306.288780@m37g2000prh.googlegroups.com> <13b1goeuhk2c12@corp.supernews.com> <1185999480.575306.65110@q3g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <13b41gfnmv2ciff@corp.supernews.com> <5hi8ifF3kqmqiU1@mid.individual.net> <g43cb3h1cs79kr34pa2vutt0ronn9q5tv6@4ax.com>`

```


<snip>
> In 1966, I was using a 360/30 with three 2311 disk drives with
> removable packs with about 7.2 megabytes per pack.  We had DOS.
>>
Yes, Clark, we moved up to DOS  not long after this. We moved off COBOL 59 
at the same time. It would have been around 67/68 (I have slightly addled 
memories of the 60s... can't think why... :-))

It was in New Zealand. The cost of these devices was prohibitive for us.

It was also our perception that these devices were still "experimental" and 
we really didn't understand how to use them. I remember one of my colleagues 
coding a COBOL "CLOSE WITH REWIND" for a disk file when we finally got to 
using them. :-)

I also remember an ICL executive at another place I worked, remarking when 
the new disk drives were demonstrated to him: "Bloody dustbin lids... 
they'll never sell..."

Ah, hindsight...:-)

Pete
```

- **References:** `<1185223084.744466.291540@22g2000hsm.googlegroups.com> <1185565019.612580.222200@e9g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <eWrqi.35$IE5.12@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net> <qKvqi.105614$wG2.60480@newsfe17.lga> <1185584321.018963.77560@e9g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <1185623435.834914.175140@l70g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <b44sa3he8fjtdlcl3fktp7990lt52a9mbp@4ax.com>`

```
On 30 Jul, 17:24, Howard Brazee <how...@brazee.net> wrote:
> On Sat, 28 Jul 2007 04:50:35 -0700, Alistair
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> (I have seen such screw-ups).

It doesn't prevent the idiot from screwing up and inserting code in
the middle of a section but it does reduce the risk of a thinking
idiot from doing so by compartmentalising code and explicitly
PERFORMing each SECTION.

I have seen code inserted into PERFORM...THRUs erroneously (a long
time ago) and personally prefer SECTIONs. If you are comfy with
PERFORM...THRU then I am happy with that.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code problems with Perform Thru Exit causes fall through

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2007-08-01T09:51:31-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sma1b39n8eidl1vdc15d1cvf2559ch9358@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1185223084.744466.291540@22g2000hsm.googlegroups.com> <1185565019.612580.222200@e9g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <eWrqi.35$IE5.12@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net> <qKvqi.105614$wG2.60480@newsfe17.lga> <1185584321.018963.77560@e9g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <1185623435.834914.175140@l70g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <b44sa3he8fjtdlcl3fktp7990lt52a9mbp@4ax.com> <1185969679.196761.242890@b79g2000hse.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Wed, 01 Aug 2007 05:01:19 -0700, Alistair
<alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:

>> How does this reduction work?
>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>PERFORMing each SECTION.

It is not obvious to me that this is reduced by use of sections.

And the most likely screw up is when the said idiot adds a new
performed paragraph at the end of the program, not noticing that that
paragraph is now part of a section.

That seems much more likely than adding that performed code to the end
of a program, not noticing that it is now part of a paragraph.

>I have seen code inserted into PERFORM...THRUs erroneously (a long
>time ago) and personally prefer SECTIONs. If you are comfy with
>PERFORM...THRU then I am happy with that.

I don't see any difference between the two in this particular case.
```

In article <1185950730.082851.47050@e16g2000pri.googlegroups.com>,
 <balaji.amarnath@gmail.com> wrote:
>On Jul 31, 4:45 pm, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:

>> >10 programs and when one main program calls the other and in the chain
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>> >correcting the code.

[snip]

>> with the parameter set up or any such discrepency)'... nice use of
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>> Is that what you are seeking to accomplish?

[snip]

>     'how can I determine, with minimal
> effort or modification, the name of the program and the paragraph-
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>mentioned requirement.

You are seeking a new and different kind of behavior out of a program; if 
you cannot modify the program it may be difficult to get this behavior out 
of it.

If you have 'lots of programs' that are unpredictably throwing errors then 
it seems reasonable to conclude that you're going to have to start 
modifying them at some point... now is as good a time as any.

>So i just want to have a uniform way of incorporating in all the
>programs so that i can make use of perl or something similar to that
>to incorporate the changes.

Now I am completely confused.  If you cannot modify the programs then 
there will be no changes to be incorporated, by use of perl or anything 
else.  Let me ask again, in a simpler fashion that will allow you to 
answer 'yes' or 'no':

What I see you asking for now is: given any error how can I determine, 
with minimal effort or modification, the name of the program and the 
paragraph-name within the program where said error occurred.

Is that what you are seeking to accomplish, yes or no?

DD
```

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2007-08-02T12:03:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f8sh5v$f2a$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<1185428108.957939.304810@e9g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <1185950730.082851.47050@e16g2000pri.googlegroups.com> <f8pj60$qbt$1@reader2.panix.com> <1186026133.418989.319650@g12g2000prg.googlegroups.com>`

```
In article <1186026133.418989.319650@g12g2000prg.googlegroups.com>,
 <balaji.amarnath@gmail.com> wrote:
>On Aug 1, 2:18 pm, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:

[snip]

>> What I see you asking for now is: given any error how can I determine,
>> with minimal effort or modification, the name of the program and the
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thanks and Regards

Thanks for the clarification.  Let this be broken down a bit more... 
first, to the name of the program.  Consider two kinds of errors:

1) Non-numeric data being subjected to arithmetic functions.  This 
results, in certain times and places, in a program's ABnormally ENDing and 
the program where said error occurred is rather obvious.  In an IBM 
mainframe batch environment one then consults the job's output and, 
depending on what tools are available, determines the exact statement 
where the error occurred with greater or lesser ease.

2) Numeric data being truncated in a significant manner.  Program A calls 
Program B using a field defined as PIC 9(3).  Program B was modified 
recently and contains a typographic error, a field which should have been 
PIC 9(3) was keyed as PIC 9(2).  This field is used in the manipulation of 
the passed field and the result is returned to Program A with high-order 
digit truncation.

Are you beginning to see some of the difficulties involved when a 
specification - such as the one you signed off on, above, with a 
resounding YES (caps original) - include the phrase 'any error'?

DD
```

Dear DD
     I had handled the run time exceptions using CBL_ERROR_PROC and
CBL_EXIT _PROC.
     Now let us assume the following code

     if input-variable <> host-variable
        move 203        to  return-status
        exit program
     end-if.

     if the input-variable (from the called program) and the host-
variable (one that is taken from the database) does not match the
program is supposed to exit.
     Here my task is to capture which program it is failed and the
paragraph name enabling easy identification of the source of error.

     I had used the following code to display the paragraph name and
the program name

     SOURCE-COMPUTER.  xxx WITH DEBUGGING MODE.

     DECLARATIVES.
     TRACE-DEBUG SECTION.
       USE FOR DEBUGGING ON ALL PROCEDURES.
     TRACE-CHECK.
       IF RETURN-STATUS IS GREATER THAN 0
          DISPLAY SQLFPN-FILENAME                 // TO DISPLAY THE
PROGRAM NAME
          DISPLAY DEBUG-NAME                      // TO DISPLAY THE
PARAGRAPH NAME
       END-IF.
      END DECLARATIVES.

  and then i was trying to move SQLFPN-FILENAME and DEBUG-NAME into a
variable but the display part by itself was not coming.
  I also included the letter D in seventh column.

  Is it possible to move DEBUG-NAME into a variable.
  What could be the possible reason why it is not working.
  Is it compatibility problem and please advice which version of cobol
supports this.

Thanks
Balaji

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Tracing paragraph Names in Pro Cobol

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2007-08-02T17:49:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f8t5fc$87p$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<1185428108.957939.304810@e9g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <1186026133.418989.319650@g12g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <f8sh5v$f2a$1@reader2.panix.com> <1186061045.782065.119650@z24g2000prh.googlegroups.com>`

```
In article <1186061045.782065.119650@z24g2000prh.googlegroups.com>,
 <balaji.amarnath@gmail.com> wrote:
>On Aug 2, 5:03 pm, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:
>> In article <1186026133.418989.319...@g12g2000prg.googlegroups.com>,

[snip]

>     I had handled the run time exceptions using CBL_ERROR_PROC and
>CBL_EXIT _PROC.
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>  What could be the possible reason why it is not working.

It might be due to the fact that it *is* working... according to 
<http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/FRAMESET/IGY3LR10/CCONTENTS?DT=20020920180651>

--begin quoted text:

Debugging sections are permitted only in the outermost program; they are 
not valid in nested programs. Debugging sections are never triggered by 
procedures contained in nested programs.

--end quoted text

I don't know if your programs are nested or not... but you might want to 
check on the functions of DEBUG-ITEMS.

>  Is it compatibility problem and please advice which version of cobol
>supports this.

There are many versions of COBOL available and I do not pretend to know 
which supports what; as should be obvious I work with IBM mainframe stuff.  
The manual for the use of debugging items there may be found at 
<http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3lr10/APPENDIX1.4.1> 
and you might want to pay special attention to phrases like 'A debugging 
section executes only if both the compile-time switch and the object-time 
switch are activated.'

DD
```

<a id="t-3ce27509bd"></a>
## Thread: Baby Update — 2 msgs

### Baby Update

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2007-08-01T11:55:17-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13b1b6l7h3nod3c@corp.supernews.com>`

```
To All:





7-21-07



The baby's heart beat often skips a beat, kind of 3 beats and then a skip 
but it is not always that regular. Friday they did a fetal echo cardiogram 
and discovered that the baby's heart has 2 small holes in it.  The holes 
can easily be repaired later when the baby is stronger and they may even 
close on their own.  However they are concerned because this can be 
associated with chromosomal disorders, especially Down syndrome (trisomy 
21), trisomy 13, and trisomy 18.  Babies with Down syndrome can live but few 
babies with trisomy 13 or trisomy 18 live more than a few months.



It is too late for amniocentesis (a test for these chromosomal 
abnormalities) as there is no fluid to speak of. They could get a genetic 
sample from the blood in the cord but the expert they had that does this got 
another job and they do not want to try it. It is hard to do especially 
since the baby is moving more and there is some risk of infection.  They 
could use a needle to get a sample of the placenta.



A part of the amniotic fluid is urine from the baby.  Whenever Judith 
urinates some fluid comes out.  They collected some of this morning 
(Saturday) and will collect more, and send it for analysis.  It will contain 
cells from both Judith and the baby and they hope to test the baby's cells 
to see if it has a chromosomal anomaly and which one.



For more information on this see:



 http://womenshealth.jhmi.edu/ob-ultrasound/patients/nt_info.html#1st%20question8-1-07The results of the chromosome tests are back. It is definitely not trisomy13, trisomy 18, or trisomy 21. There are some other possible chromosomedefects but they are very rare and they usually do not test for them.  Alsobabies with those defects usually result in a miscarriage.The baby is a girl.The baby's heart rate has dropped down twice now probably due to compressionof the umbilical chord. The amniotic fluid usually protects the chord but ofcourse we have no fluid. When the chord is compressed the baby is notgetting enough oxygen and if the heart rate stays down too long the willdeliver the baby via c-section. Judith and the baby are now being monitoredfor the baby's heart rate and for contractions 24 hours a day.  They also dosonograms and for the baby's heart they do echocardiograms.The main problem is the development of the lungs and the birth defect.Thanks for your prayers.
```

#### ↳ Re: Baby Update

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-08-02T15:29:35+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5hd1d1F3jr6ivU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<13b1b6l7h3nod3c@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Thanks for the update, Charlie.

Thoughts are with you.

Pete
```

<a id="t-a638e3a9a6"></a>
## Thread: Paagraphs and Sections — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Paagraphs and Sections

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2007-08-01T17:15:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4d3si.10788$8M3.7822@fe07.news.easynews.com>`

```
I have only skimmed all the current notes, but was wondering if anyone had 
posted something that said that one of the structures actually ABSOLUTELY 
prevented some type of "bad coding" technique or whether one "guaranteed" easier 
to maintain code.

It has always been my position that there are "good" styles (sets of rules) that 
a programmer or shop could set up that would produce "easier to maintain and 
less error-prone" (non-spaghetti) code.  Once a programmer was familiar with 
such a style, they could pick up a "new" program and understand its structure 
and maintain it in a comparable way.

I can describe such "good" (IMHO) techniques using either Paragraphs-only or 
Sections (with or without paragraphs other than exit paragraphs).  I can also 
tell (for either type) how a "bad" programmer could mess up such a program.

I guess this note is just because it always surprises me that some of those 
posting on this (reoccurring) topic always SOUND as if they think they will 
"convince" someone who uses (and is used to) one approach to change their mind - 
and this certainly never happens.
```

#### ↳ Re: Paagraphs and Sections

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2007-08-01T11:41:24-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2ch1b31lkq2urfcem124inoqh96k3t635p@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<4d3si.10788$8M3.7822@fe07.news.easynews.com>`

```
On Wed, 01 Aug 2007 17:15:44 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>I guess this note is just because it always surprises me that some of those 
>posting on this (reoccurring) topic always SOUND as if they think they will 
>"convince" someone who uses (and is used to) one approach to change their mind - 
>and this certainly never happens.

How many of us have our current style 3-4 decades ago?   I know I was
convinced to changed - more than once.

Were you persuaded to change?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Paagraphs and Sections

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2007-08-06T12:43:43-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<j5Jti.5330$2v5.206@bignews2.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<4d3si.10788$8M3.7822@fe07.news.easynews.com> <2ch1b31lkq2urfcem124inoqh96k3t635p@4ax.com>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>
> How many of us have our current style 3-4 decades ago?   I know I was
> convinced to changed - more than once.
>
> Were you persuaded to change?

Hello Howard. My style of coding COBOL hasn't changed noticeably in 20
years, but I spent the previous 20 years 'perfecting' it. I'm still working on
my coding styles for newer environments (e.g. Visual Studio 2005) and for
environments I haven't done a lot of production code in.
```

#### ↳ Re: Paagraphs and Sections

- **From:** Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2007-08-06T15:47:16-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1186440436.601560.270300@k79g2000hse.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<4d3si.10788$8M3.7822@fe07.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<4d3si.10788$8M3.7822@fe07.news.easynews.com>`

```
On 1 Aug, 18:15, "William M. Klein" <wmkl...@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
> I have only skimmed all the current notes, but was wondering if anyone had
> posted something that said that one of the structures actually ABSOLUTELY
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>  wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

I may come across as being somewhat zealous, dictatorial and right
wing (those are my good points). I do not believe it is possible to
change the points of view of the members of this forum but it does not
constitute a waste of time or effort. I think that after one has been
coding a few years then there will be a style with which one is happy
and there will be little incentive to change. As a former contractor,
I have had to adapt my style to that of other institutions and pride
myself, in that on most occasions, bar one notable exception, I have
been able to adapt that style to whatever style the program had been
written in and make my corrections/changes/enhancements appear
seamless. That is good style (IMHO).
```

<a id="t-7f4ca5129d"></a>
## Thread: MicroFocus .INT & .GNT — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MicroFocus .INT & .GNT

- **From:** "Paul H" <NoSpamphobergNoSpam@att.net>
- **Date:** 2007-08-03T13:45:02-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_IKsi.17725$lZ7.7146@newsfe20.lga>`

```
Does anyone remember the days of .INT and .GNT code?  We created a .INT if 
the computing was minimal but quick loading for responsive 
screen-to-new-screen speed was important.  A .GNT loaded slower, but 
executed like the wind, so billing runs, etc., were .GNT.  Commonly used 
modules, for example to keep files open from program to program, were 
usually .GNT because batch runs also used them.  Do equivalent techniques 
apply to current NetExpress .DLL's?  Can one create interpretive vs. 
compiled code?  And run the mix cooperatively?  And control the "load and 
stay resident" characteristics of code to used all during the on-line day? 
TIA, Paul
```

#### ↳ Re: MicroFocus .INT & .GNT

- **From:** Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-08-03T16:10:11-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1186182611.947965.59510@i13g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<_IKsi.17725$lZ7.7146@newsfe20.lga>`
- **References:** `<_IKsi.17725$lZ7.7146@newsfe20.lga>`

```
On Aug 4, 6:45 am, "Paul H" <NoSpamphobergNoS...@att.net> wrote:
> Does anyone remember the days of .INT and .GNT code?  We created a .INT if
> the computing was minimal but quick loading for responsive
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> TIA, Paul

You should be able to control the way the code is generated with the
OPT command. IIRC:

OPT(0) == .int
OPT(1) == .gnt
OPT(n) == various stages of additional optimisation.

When I thought it mattered (2MHz 8085 to 12MHz 386s) the procedure
division of .INTs was about 1/3rd the size of .GNT (Data was same
size) but ran about 1/3rd the speed.

The main advantage of .INT was that the run-time system was shared
code on MP/M and Concurrent-DOS so was only loaded once for all users
and the .INT was smaller so a 1Mbyte RAM 8086 or 80286 machine could
run 4 users.

But that was a quarter century ago.
```

#### ↳ Re: MicroFocus .INT & .GNT

- **From:** "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2007-08-03T17:57:56-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<46B36CA4.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`
- **References:** `<_IKsi.17725$lZ7.7146@newsfe20.lga>`

```
>>> On 8/3/2007 at 12:45 PM, in message
<_IKsi.17725$lZ7.7146@newsfe20.lga>,
Paul H<NoSpamphobergNoSpam@att.net> wrote:
> Does anyone remember the days of .INT and .GNT code?  We created a .INT 
> if 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> day? 

For those of use who aren't as familiar with Micro Focus COBOL, can you
explain what .INT and .GNT files are, and how they are different from each
other?

(Not being snide.  I really want to know.)

Frank
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MicroFocus .INT & .GNT

- **From:** Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-08-03T17:22:29-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1186186949.968857.247320@q3g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<46B36CA4.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`
- **References:** `<_IKsi.17725$lZ7.7146@newsfe20.lga> <46B36CA4.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`

```
On Aug 4, 11:57 am, "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbr...@efirstbank.com>
wrote:
> >>> On 8/3/2007 at 12:45 PM, in message
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> (Not being snide.  I really want to know.)

.INT is 'intermediate' code which is like USCD Pascal or Java byte
code. It is machine independent and executed via a run-time
interpreter or VM.

.GNT is 'generated' which is machine code and thus different for each
machine type.

With old Level II Cobol there were two different programs that did
these formats. The compiler produced .INT and then this was fed into a
'generator' which created the .GNT.

On some systems (MS-DOS, OS/2, Windows) the code can be bundled with
appropriate library and run-time code into .EXE executables and .DLL
shared libraries (actually these are .EXE too on MS-DOS). The code
segments within these can still be byte code or machine code by
specifying the OPT compiler command.
```

<a id="t-7395719a47"></a>
## Thread: nested programs — 3 msgs

### nested programs

- **From:** "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2007-08-03T17:54:25-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<46B36BD1.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`

```
I've be reading but not commenting on this whole thing about sections versus
paragraphs, etc., and now a bit of talk about nested programs.  So, who all
out there uses nested programs.  I've done a bit of 'experimenting' with
them, and I'm really not quite happy with them.  In order to put inject some
reality in to my discussion I am going to post the major parts of an actual
production program that uses them.  I have stripped out most of the
business-oriented specifics, for two reasons.  One, I'd probably get in
trouble for leaking 'trade secrets' <g>.  And two, they just clutter up the
point I am trying to make.

There's still a lot of code left, probably too much, but I couldn't see what
else to eliminate without making the example useless.  So here it is:

 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.                                         
 PROGRAM-ID.    CICSV2K.                                          
 AUTHOR.        FRANK SWARBRICK.                                  
 DATE-WRITTEN.  APRIL - MAY 2006.                                 
 INSTALLATION.  FIRSTBANK DATA CORP.                              
*                                                                 
 DATA DIVISION.                                                   
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.                                         
 COPY GLOBALWS.                                                   
/                                                                 
 01  GLOBAL-WORKING-STORAGE      IS GLOBAL.                       
     05  SW-LAST-CALL            PIC X       VALUE 'N'.           
         88  LAST-CALL                       VALUE 'Y'.           
     05  MSC-DATES.                                               
         10  MSC-MODYYR-X.                                        
             15  MSC-MODYYR      PIC 999999.                      
             15                  REDEFINES MSC-MODYYR.            
                 20  MSC-MO      PIC 99.                          
                 20  MSC-DY      PIC 99.                          
                 20  MSC-YR      PIC 99.                       
         10  MSC-JULDATE         PIC 9(5).                     
         10  MSC-DAYS            PIC 9(5).                     
         10  MSC-DYWK            PIC 9.                        
     05  CURR-CCYYMMDD           PIC 9(8).                     
     05                          REDEFINES CURR-CCYYMMDD.      
         10  CURR-CCYY           PIC 9(4).                     
         10  CURR-MO             PIC 9(2).                     
         10  CURR-DY             PIC 9(2).                     
/                                                              
 COPY XML1V2K REPLACING ==OCCURS 1== BY ====.                  
/                                                              
 COPY CVCMUCHR REPLACING                                       
    == DEPENDING ON CHR-DEPENDING ==                           
 BY == DEPENDING ON CHR-DEPENDING INDEXED BY CAI ==            
    == CHR-TRAN-ACTIVITY       OCCURS 15. ==                   
 BY == CHR-TRAN-ACTIVITY OCCURS 15 INDEXED BY CTI. ==          
    == CHR-USER-RECORD. == BY == CHR-USER-RECORD IS GLOBAL. ==.
/                                                              
 01.                                                           
     05  CVASSUBR-POINTER        PROCEDURE-POINTER.            
     05  FSUB                    PIC S9(4) COMP.               
/                                                                 
 LINKAGE SECTION.                                                 
 COPY DFHEIBLK REPLACING == EIBLK. == BY == dfheiblk global. ==.  
                                                                  
 01  DFHCOMMAREA                 PIC X(2000) GLOBAL.              
 COPY VIEWCOMM REPLACING                                          
 ==CICSVIEW-COMMAREA.==                                           
 BY ==CICSVIEW-COMMAREA GLOBAL REDEFINES DFHCOMMAREA. ==          
 ==01  CICSVIEW-COMMAREA-LENGTH    PIC S9(4) COMP VALUE +2000.==  
 BY == ==.                                                        
                                                                  
 COPY CICSCWA REPLACING                                           
     == COMMON-WORK-AREA. == BY == COMMON-WORK-AREA IS GLOBAL. ==.
/                                                                 
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.                                              
     MOVE ZERO                   TO RETURN-CODE                   
     PERFORM HOUSEKEEPING                                         
     PERFORM MAINLINE                                             
     IF GLOBAL-ERROR                                              
     AND COMM-VIEW-RETURN-MSG NOT > SPACES                        
         CALL 'GET-ERROR-INFO'                                    
     END-IF                                                       
     EXEC CICS RETURN                              
     END-EXEC                                      
     GOBACK.                                       
                                                   
 HOUSEKEEPING.                                     
     EXEC CICS ADDRESS                             
         EIB (ADDRESS OF DFHEIBLK)                 
         COMMAREA (ADDRESS OF DFHCOMMAREA)         
         CWA (ADDRESS OF COMMON-WORK-AREA)         
     END-EXEC                                      
     EXEC CICS HANDLE CONDITION                    
         ERROR (CICS-ERROR)                        
     END-EXEC                                      
     SET CVASSUBR-POINTER TO ENTRY 'CVASSUBR'      
     EXIT.                                         
                                                   
 MAINLINE.                                         
     PERFORM VARYING FSUB FROM 1 BY 1              
               UNTIL FSUB > COMM-VIEW-MAX-FUNCTIONS
                  OR GLOBAL-ERROR                  
         CALL COMM-FUNC-REQUESTED(FSUB)            
         ON EXCEPTION                              
             STRING 'ERROR CALLING ROUTINE: '             
                    COMM-FUNC-REQUESTED(FSUB)             
               DELIMITED BY SIZE INTO COMM-VIEW-RETURN-MSG
             SET GLOBAL-ERROR TO TRUE                     
         END-CALL                                         
     END-PERFORM                                          
     EXIT.                                                
                                                          
 CICS-ERROR.                                              
     CALL 'CICS-ERROR'                                    
     CALL 'GET-ERROR-INFO'                                
     EXEC CICS RETURN                                     
     END-EXEC                                             
     EXIT.                                                
**********************************************************
 ID DIVISION.                                             
 PROGRAM-ID. 'acctCardListByCust'.                        
 DATA DIVISION.                                           
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.                                 
 01.                                                      
     05  RSUB                    PIC S9(4) COMP.          
     05  BRCH-ACCT               PIC 9(10).                
     05                          REDEFINES BRCH-ACCT.      
         10  BRCH                PIC 9(3).                 
         10  ACCT                PIC 9(7).                 
     05  HOLD-EXPR-DATE          PIC 9999.                 
     05  REDEFINES HOLD-EXPR-DATE.                         
         10  HOLD-EXPR-YR        PIC 99.                   
         10  HOLD-EXPR-MO        PIC 99.                   
 01  SWITCHES.                                     
     05  MORE-CARDS-SW           PIC X.            
         88  MORE-CARDS                  VALUE 'Y'.
         88  NO-MORE-CARDS               VALUE 'N'.
/                                                  
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.                               
     MOVE SPACES                 TO V2K-COPY       
     MOVE 'N'                    TO RESULTS        
     MOVE COMM-VIEW-BRANCH-NBR   TO BRCH           
     MOVE COMM-VIEW-ACCT-NBR     TO ACCT           
     PERFORM GET-CARDS-FOR-ACCOUNT                 
     IF NO-GLOBAL-ERROR                            
         PERFORM CLEANUP                           
     END-IF                                        
                                                    
 GET-CARDS-FOR-ACCOUNT.                             
     PERFORM NEXT-CARDHOLDER-RECORD                 
     PERFORM UNTIL NO-MORE-CARDS                    
                OR GLOBAL-ERROR                     
         PERFORM HANDLE-RECORD                      
         PERFORM NEXT-CARDHOLDER-RECORD             
     END-PERFORM                                    
     EXIT.                                          
                                                    
 NEXT-CARDHOLDER-RECORD.                            
     CALL 'NEXTCARDHOLDERRECORD' USING BRCH-ACCT    
                                       MORE-CARDS-SW
     EXIT.                                          
                                                    
 HANDLE-RECORD.                                     
*    do stuff here
     PERFORM OUTPUT-ROUTINE                  
                                             
     EXIT.                                   
                                             
 OUTPUT-ROUTINE.                             
     CALL 'OUTPUT-ROUTINE'                   
     IF GLOBAL-ERROR                         
         EXIT PROGRAM                        
     END-IF                                  
     EXIT.                                   
                                             
 CLEANUP.                                    
*    NEED TO BUILD XML DOC EVEN IF NO RESULTS
     IF RESULTS = 'N'                        
         MOVE SPACE              TO RESULTS  
         PERFORM OUTPUT-ROUTINE              
     END-IF                                  
     MOVE HIGH-VALUES TO V2K-COPY 
     SET  LAST-CALL   TO TRUE     
     PERFORM OUTPUT-ROUTINE       
     EXIT.                        
                                  
 END PROGRAM 'acctCardListByCust'.
**************************************
 ID DIVISION.                                             
 PROGRAM-ID. 'cardLimits'.                                
 DATA DIVISION.                                           
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.                                 
 01.                                                      
     05  CARD-PAN.                                        
         10  NBR                 PIC 9(16).               
         10                      PIC X(3)    VALUE SPACES.
     05  CARD-REC-SW             PIC X.                   
         88  CARD-REC-FOUND              VALUE 'Y'.       
         88  CARD-REC-NOT-FOUND          VALUE 'Y'.       
 01  READCARDHOLDERREC           PIC X(32)   VALUE        
     'READCARDHOLDERREC'.                                 
/                                                         
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.                               
     MOVE SPACES                 TO V2K-COPY       
     MOVE COMM-VIEW-CCARD-NBR    TO NBR OF CARD-PAN
     PERFORM GET-LIMITS                            
     PERFORM CLEANUP                               
                                                   
     EXIT PROGRAM.                                 
                                                   
 GET-LIMITS.                                       
     PERFORM READ-CARDHOLDER-FILE                  
     IF CARD-REC-FOUND                             
         PERFORM HANDLE-RECORD                     
     END-IF                                        
     PERFORM OUTPUT-ROUTINE                        
     EXIT.                                         
                                                   
 HANDLE-RECORD.                                    
     PERFORM GET-CARD-LIMITS     
     IF CHR-TYPE-BVIS            
         PERFORM GET-BVIS-LIMITS 
     ELSE                        
         PERFORM GET-APPL-LIMITS 
     END-IF                      
     EXIT.                       

 GET-CARD-LIMITS.
*    Do stuff here
     EXIT.

 GET-BVIS-LIMITS.
*    Do stuff here
     EXIT.

 GET-APPL-LIMITS.
*    Do stuff here
     EXIT.

 READ-CARDHOLDER-FILE.                      
     MOVE 'CARDSYS' TO SUBROUTINE           
     CALL SUBROUTINE USING READCARDHOLDERREC
                           CARD-PAN         
                           CARD-REC-SW      
                           CHR-USER-RECORD  
     IF RETURN-CODE > ZERO                  
         SET GLOBAL-ERROR TO TRUE           
         MOVE 'N' TO CARD-REC-SW            
         EXIT PROGRAM                       
     END-IF                                 
     EXIT.                                  
                                            
 OUTPUT-ROUTINE.                            
     CALL 'OUTPUT-ROUTINE'                  
     IF GLOBAL-ERROR                        
         EXIT PROGRAM                       
     END-IF                                 
     EXIT.                                  
                                            
 CLEANUP.                                   
     MOVE HIGH-VALUES TO V2K-COPY           
     SET  LAST-CALL   TO TRUE               
     PERFORM OUTPUT-ROUTINE          
     EXIT.                           
                                     
 END PROGRAM 'cardLimits'.           
*************************************
 ID DIVISION.                                             
 PROGRAM-ID. 'cardTranHistory'.                           
 DATA DIVISION.                                           
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.                                 
 01.                                                      
     05  CARD-PAN.                                        
         10  NBR                 PIC 9(16).               
         10                      PIC X(3)    VALUE SPACES.
     05  CARD-REC-SW             PIC X.                   
         88  CARD-REC-FOUND              VALUE 'Y'.       
         88  CARD-REC-NOT-FOUND          VALUE 'Y'.       
     05  ANSI-CODE               PIC 9(6).                
                                                          
 01  READCARDHOLDERREC           PIC X(32)   VALUE        
     'READCARDHOLDERREC'.                                 
/                                                         
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.                                   
     MOVE SPACES                 TO V2K-COPY           
     MOVE 'N'                    TO RESULTS            
     MOVE COMM-VIEW-CCARD-NBR    TO NBR OF CARD-PAN    
     PERFORM GET-TRAN-HISTORY                          
     PERFORM CLEANUP                                   
                                                       
     EXIT PROGRAM.                                     
                                                       
 GET-TRAN-HISTORY.                                     
     PERFORM READ-CARDHOLDER-FILE                      
     IF CARD-REC-FOUND                                 
         PERFORM HANDLE-RECORD                         
     END-IF                                            
     EXIT.                                             
                                                       
 HANDLE-RECORD.                                        
     PERFORM VARYING CTI FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL CTI > 15    
                                        OR GLOBAL-ERROR
         IF CHR-TRAN-DATE(CTI) NUMERIC                 
         AND CHR-TRAN-DATE(CTI) POSITIVE               
             MOVE SPACE          TO RESULTS            
             PERFORM HANDLE-TRANSACTION                
         END-IF                                        
     END-PERFORM                                       
     EXIT.                                             
                                                       
 HANDLE-TRANSACTION.                                   
*    Do stuff here
 READ-CARDHOLDER-FILE.                       
     MOVE 'CARDSYS' TO SUBROUTINE            
     CALL SUBROUTINE USING READCARDHOLDERREC 
                           CARD-PAN          
                           CARD-REC-SW       
                           CHR-USER-RECORD   
     IF RETURN-CODE > ZERO                   
         SET GLOBAL-ERROR TO TRUE            
         MOVE 'N' TO CARD-REC-SW             
         EXIT PROGRAM                        
     END-IF                                  
     EXIT.                                   
                                             
 OUTPUT-ROUTINE.                             
     CALL 'OUTPUT-ROUTINE'                   
     IF GLOBAL-ERROR                         
         EXIT PROGRAM                        
     END-IF                                  
     EXIT.                                   
                                             
 CLEANUP.                                    
*    NEED TO BUILD XML DOC EVEN IF NO RESULTS
     IF RESULTS = 'N'                        
         MOVE SPACE              TO RESULTS
         PERFORM OUTPUT-ROUTINE            
     END-IF                                
     MOVE HIGH-VALUES TO V2K-COPY          
     SET  LAST-CALL   TO TRUE              
     PERFORM OUTPUT-ROUTINE                
     EXIT.                                 
 END PROGRAM 'cardTranHistory'.            
*******************************************
 TITLE 'NEXTCARDHOLDERRECORD'                                 
 ID DIVISION.                                                 
 PROGRAM-ID. 'NEXTCARDHOLDERRECORD' IS COMMON.                
 DATA DIVISION.                                               
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.                                     
 01.                                                          
     05  CARDHOLDER-XREF         PIC X(8)    VALUE 'CVSXRF  '.
     05  BROWSE-STARTED          PIC X   VALUE 'N'.           
     05  SEARCH-SYSTEM           PIC X.                       
         88  SEARCH-DDA                  VALUE '1'.           
         88  SEARCH-SAV                  VALUE '2'.           
                                                              
 01  READCARDHOLDERREC           PIC X(32)   VALUE            
     'READCARDHOLDERREC'.                                     
                                                              
 01  SWITCHES.                                                
     05  NEXT-CARD-SW            PIC X.                       
         88  NEXT-CARD-FOUND             VALUE 'Y'.           
         88  NEXT-CARD-NOT-FOUND         VALUE 'N'.           
                                                              
 LINKAGE SECTION.                                    
 01  ACCOUNT                     PIC 9(10).          
 01  RECORDS-FLAG                PIC X.              
     88  MORE-RECORDS                    VALUE 'Y'.  
     88  NO-MORE-RECORDS                 VALUE 'N'.  
                                                     
 PROCEDURE DIVISION USING ACCOUNT                    
                          RECORDS-FLAG.              
                                                     
     SET NEXT-CARD-NOT-FOUND TO TRUE                 
     IF BROWSE-STARTED = 'N'                         
         SET SEARCH-DDA TO TRUE                      
         PERFORM START-ATM-XREF                      
     END-IF                                          
     PERFORM UNTIL NEXT-CARD-FOUND OR NO-MORE-RECORDS
         PERFORM NEXT-XREF                           
     END-PERFORM                                     
                                                     
     EXIT PROGRAM.                                   
                                                     
 NEXT-XREF.
*    Do stuff here
     EXIT.

 START-ATM-XREF.
*    Do stuff here
     EXIT.

 RESTART-ATM-XREF.
*    Do stuff here
     EXIT.

 READ-CARD-XREF-FILE. 
*    Do stuff here
     EXIT.

 END-ATM-XREF.
*    Do stuff here
     EXIT.

 READ-CARDHOLDER-FILE.                      
     MOVE 'CARDSYS' TO SUBROUTINE           
     CALL SUBROUTINE USING READCARDHOLDERREC
                           CHR-XREF-PAN     
                           NEXT-CARD-SW     
                           CHR-USER-RECORD  
     IF RETURN-CODE > ZERO                  
         SET GLOBAL-ERROR TO TRUE           
         MOVE 'N' TO NEXT-CARD-SW           
         EXIT PROGRAM                       
     END-IF                                 
     EXIT.                                  
                                            
 END PROGRAM 'NEXTCARDHOLDERRECORD'.        
**********************************
 ID DIVISION.                                                 
 PROGRAM-ID. 'TERMINAL-LOCATION' IS COMMON.                   
 DATA DIVISION.                                               
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.                                     
 01.                                                          
     05  DEVICE-CONTROL-FILE     PIC X(8)    VALUE 'CVSDCF  '.
                                                              
 COPY CVDHDCFR.                                               
/                                                             
 LINKAGE SECTION.                                             
 01  TERMID                      PIC X(8).                    
 01  LOCATION                    PIC X(25).                   
 PROCEDURE DIVISION USING TERMID                              
                          LOCATION.                           
*    Look up TERMID and return LOCATION                                    
     EXIT PROGRAM.               
 END PROGRAM 'TERMINAL-LOCATION'.
**********************************
 ID DIVISION.                                                 
 PROGRAM-ID. 'OUTPUT-ROUTINE' IS COMMON.                      
 DATA DIVISION.                                               
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.                                     
 01.                                                          
     05  FIRST-TIME              PIC X       VALUE 'Y'.       
     05  CRD-SOURCE-NAME         PIC X(8)    VALUE 'XML1V2K '.
     05  XML-CURR-DOCUMENT-LEN   PIC S9(8) BINARY.            
                                                              
 LINKAGE SECTION.                                             
 01  XML-DOCUMENT                PIC X(10000).                
                                                              
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.                                          
     IF FIRST-TIME = 'Y'                                      
         MOVE 'N'                TO FIRST-TIME                
         COMPUTE XML-CURR-DOCUMENT-LEN                        
            = FUNCTION LENGTH(XML-DOCUMENT)                   
     END-IF                                                   
     MOVE 'COB2XML' TO SUBROUTINE                             
     CALL SUBROUTINE                                          
       USING BY REFERENCE V2K-COPY                            
             BY CONTENT   LENGTH OF V2K-COPY                  
             BY REFERENCE CRD-SOURCE-NAME                     
                          ADDRESS OF XML-DOCUMENT    
                          XML-CURR-DOCUMENT-LEN      
                          COMM-VIEW-RETURN-MSG       
     IF RETURN-CODE = ZERO                           
         MOVE 'SENDDOC' TO SUBROUTINE                
         CALL SUBROUTINE USING COMM-VIEW-OUTPUT-LEN  
                               XML-DOCUMENT          
                               XML-CURR-DOCUMENT-LEN 
                               SW-LAST-CALL          
                               COMM-VIEW-CONN-ID     
                               COMM-VIEW-MSG-ID      
     ELSE                                            
         SET GLOBAL-ERROR TO TRUE                    
     END-IF                                          
     EXIT PROGRAM.                                   
 END PROGRAM 'OUTPUT-ROUTINE'.                       
*****************************************************
 ID DIVISION.                                     
 PROGRAM-ID. 'CICS-ERROR' IS COMMON.              
 DATA DIVISION.                                   
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.                         
 01  ERROR-FUNCTION              PIC X(32).       
                                                  
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.                              
     MOVE 'ERRINFO'              TO SUBROUTINE    
     MOVE 'CICS-ERROR-INFO'      TO ERROR-FUNCTION
     CALL SUBROUTINE USING       ERROR-FUNCTION   
                     BY CONTENT  EIBRESP          
                                 EIBRESP2         
                                 EIBFN            
                                 EIBRSRCE         
     SET GLOBAL-ERROR TO TRUE                     
     EXIT PROGRAM.                                
                                                  
 END PROGRAM 'CICS-ERROR'.                        
**************************************************
 TITLE 'GET-ERROR-INFO'                           
 ID DIVISION.                                     
 PROGRAM-ID. 'GET-ERROR-INFO' IS COMMON.          
 DATA DIVISION.                                   
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.                                 
 01  ERROR-AREA.                                          
     05  ERROR-FUNCTION          PIC X(32).               
     05  ERROR-LINE-LEN          PIC 9(2).                
     05  ERROR-LINE-CNT          PIC 9(2).                
                                                          
 LINKAGE SECTION.                                         
 COPY ERRINFOC.                                           
                                                          
 01  ERROR-LINES.                                         
     05  ERROR-LINE              PIC X(80) OCCURS 1 TIMES.
                                                          
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.                                      
     MOVE LENGTH OF ERROR-LINE(1) TO ERROR-LINE-LEN       
     COMPUTE ERROR-LINE-CNT = LENGTH OF ERROR-LINES       
                            / LENGTH OF ERROR-LINE(1)     
     MOVE 'ERRINFO'              TO SUBROUTINE            
     MOVE 'GET-ERROR-INFO'       TO ERROR-FUNCTION        
     CALL SUBROUTINE USING ERROR-AREA                     
                           ADDRESS OF ERROR-INFO          
                           ADDRESS OF ERROR-INFO-AREA     
                           ADDRESS OF ERROR-LINES         
     MOVE ERROR-LINE (1)         TO COMM-VIEW-RETURN-MSG
     SET GLOBAL-ERROR TO TRUE                           
     EXIT PROGRAM.                                      
                                                        
 END PROGRAM 'GET-ERROR-INFO'.                          
********************************************************
 END PROGRAM CICSV2K.                                   

The end.  L:-)

Here's kind of an 'outline' of the program.

Main program, contains all other programs:
  'CICSV2K'

Nested programs that can be called by the main program but not by each
other:
  'acctCardListByCust'
  'cardLimits'
  'cardTranHistory'

COMMON programs that can be called by all other programs within this
compilation unit:
  'NEXTCARDHOLDERRECORD'
  'TERMINAL-LOCATION'
  'OUTPUT-ROUTINE'
  'CICS-ERROR'
  'GET-ERROR-INFO'

Finally, there are some actual 'external' subroutines (source code not
posted):
  'CARDSYS'
  'CEESITST'
  'CVASJULD'
  'COB2XML'
  'SENDDOC'
  'ERRINFO'


Notes of note...

The three primary nested programs perform three primary functions that can
be requested by the caller (meaning the program linking (this is CICS) to
CICSV2K).  You may notice that none of the three have any parameters passed
in.  All of the parameters they use are declared as being GLOBAL.  Really,
there is only one 'parameter', as such, that being the COMMAREA (VIEWCOMM
copybook).

I could instead have passed the commarea as a parameter to each of the 3
primary nested programs, and I'm sure there are those who think that I
should have.  The main reason I did not is because I just didn't see the
point of having the same copybook in four different places.  It just bloats
the compile listing and simply did not seem like a justifiable thing to do.

To further justify myself, try looking at it as if it were an OO program. 
Think of all of the nested programs as being methods, with the main program
procedure division kind of being a 'driver' method.  (Though in real OO it
wouldn't really be needed.  You'd just invoke the three primary methods
directly.)  Anyway, going with this thinking I see the "global" working
storage as performing the same function as would "Object" storage (in OO
COBOL that would be the WORKING-STORAGE section defined in the OBJECT; not
as a parameter or local-storage to a method.)

Of course my justification falls apart a bit there because in true OO, as I
said before, one probably wouldn't have the 'driver' method at all.  But
lets imagine, for whatever reason, one did.  So, as I said, it would (I
think!) be quite acceptable to have the 'commarea' as instance (object)
fields, rather than passing them in linkage.

Now we get to where I'm a bit less justified, but I still did it for the
same reason.  I have some other fields defined as GLOBAL.  The CVCMUCHR
copybook (01  CHR-USER-RECORD), for example.  Again, I could have (should
have?) defined these in the local working-storage for each nested program. 
But I simply couldn't see the point.  All it would do is take up more
memory, more space on the compile output, etc., and I wouldn't (in this
case, at least) have gained any real advantage.

Anyway, the 'NEXTCARDHOLDERRECORD' is more of a traditional called nested
program, in that I pass two parameters.  But again, I'm not passing the
cardholder record itself, again using the GLOBAL record.

One of my biggest disappointments with this whole process is how I am
handling the call to 'OUTPUT-ROUTINE'.  Rather than simply calling it from
several places, I am instead performing a paragraph which does the call. 
I'll show it again:

 OUTPUT-ROUTINE.          
     CALL 'OUTPUT-ROUTINE'
     IF GLOBAL-ERROR      
         EXIT PROGRAM     
     END-IF               
     EXIT.                

Obviously, the reason I am doing this is because if the error-checking logic
that follows it.  But it really annoyed me that I had to duplicate logic in
order to not duplicate logic!  In other words, had I not insisted on doing
nested programs I could have simply had what is now the OUTPUT-ROUTINE
program as a paragraph that I could have performed from multiple places!  I
did something similar for the call to the external subroutine CARDSYS, for
function READCARDHOLDERREC.

In the end, would I do something like this again?  Hmm, I'm not sure.  Very
possibly not.  External subroutines are one thing, and are very useful
because the same logic can be called from many programs.  Nested programs
don't have the same benefit.  They can be called only from the programs in
which they are nested (or, if COMMON, from other nested programs within the
same compilation unit.)  There are only two real benefits I can see that
nested programs have:
1) Locality of reference.  Having a variable declared closer to where it's
being used.  Personally, I would like COBOL to have "paragraph local"
variables.  Especially for subscripts for use within loops.

2) Passing of 'like' but actually different fields.  In other words:

CALL SUBROUTINE USING FIELD-1, FIELD-2
CALL SUBROUTINE USING FIELD-3, FIELD-4

rather than
MOVE FIELD-1 TO A-PARA-DATA-IN
PERFORM A-PARAGRAPH
MOVE A-PARA-DATA-OUT TO FIELD-2
MOVE FIELD-3 TO A-PARA-DATA-IN
PERFORM A-PARAGRAPH
MOVE A-PARA-DATA-OUT TO FIELD-4


A-PARAGRAPH.
*  input being A-PARA-DATA-IN
*  output being A-PARA-DATA-OUT

But in the end, is really worth it?  Again I am not convinced.
What I am somewhat convinced of is that it appears that the whole idea of
nested programs was added to the COBOL 85 standard prior to anyone actually
implementing them and working with them.  (Much of the COBOL 2002 standard
appears to have been done this way as well.)  The idea is quite sound.  But
I'm not sold on the implementation.  One thing that may have made it work
better would have been if the TYPEDEF clause had existed in COBOL 85.  Had
this been the case I would have bad no hesitation in making the COMMAREA
structure a TYPE, and passing it around when necessary.

Anyway, enough rambling for now.  Comments, criticisms, and especially
suggestions on how to make this program better are all welcome, as long (as
they are reasonable!).

Frank
```

#### ↳ Re: nested programs

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2007-08-03T18:40:35-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9SdnY9dWc2VVi7bnZ2dnUVZ_rOpnZ2d@comcast.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<46B36BD1.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`
- **References:** `<46B36BD1.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`

```
Frank Swarbrick wrote:
> I've be reading but not commenting on this whole thing about sections versus
> paragraphs, etc., and now a bit of talk about nested programs.  So, who all
> out there uses nested programs.

I've used them in a couple of places.  I really like the separation of 
data.  However, on the Unisys platform, the network database stuff isn't 
visible from them, which is kind of a downer.

The biggest program I did was an online program that did input 
validation for nearly every batch program in the system.  That way, if 
the input was bad, we wouldn't waste the cycles starting the program, 
only to have it reject.  Plus, it gave the users instant feedback, as 
opposed to the run getting queued, them going home for the day, then 
coming in the next morning to get their reject.

Anyway, each run has a TRansaction Identifier Code (TRIC).  There was a 
paragraph called "Program-Specific-Edits" that was basically one big 
evaluate...

     evaluate wsInputTric
       when "ABC"
          call "ABC-Edits"
       when "DEF"
          call "DEF-Edits"
       ...
     end-evaluate

For those that needed network database validation, I had to put a 
"perform ABC-DB-Edits" after the call, and in some cases, I had to pass 
parameters to the subroutines.  They modified GLOBAL variables, but 
their work variables were isolated.  The program ran pretty efficiently, 
plus it introduced several other people to nested subprograms, as people 
could check it out and add edits for their programs.
```

#### ↳ Re: nested programs

- **From:** Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2007-08-03T18:10:41-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1186189841.322744.257320@22g2000hsm.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<46B36BD1.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`
- **References:** `<46B36BD1.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`

```
On 4 Aug, 00:54, "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbr...@efirstbank.com>
wrote:
> I've be reading but not commenting on this whole thing about sections versus
> paragraphs, etc., and now a bit of talk about nested programs.  So, who all
…[171 more quoted lines elided]…
> read more �...

Whether you use nesting or not is up to you and your design authority.
Back in Doc's golden dayse I had to debug a multi-tiered nested
program where the abending sub/program was down several levels. Before
Abend-aid it was a real pain. However, writing distinct programs or
modules to handle distinct functionality (eg in-stream data input of
pagination and printing) is wise as you don't need to re-invent the
wheel with each new program. But adding a new spoke to that wheel can
be problematic too.
```

## Thread: [OT] Laugh-Generating Situations — 9 msgs

### [OT] Laugh-Generating Situations

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2007-08-04T14:36:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f922sp$d71$1@reader2.panix.com>`

```

All righty... there's been some talk 'round these parts of late as to what 
is the criterion which makes something 'humo(u)rous'.  Having spent, in my 
days, a fair amount of time in any of the four (or more) positions of a 
joke:

As teller
As audience
As member of group being spoken of
As member of other-than group being spoken of

... I feel eminently qualified to supply what any right-thinking person 
will be able to conclude is The Ultimate Definition.

It is, in essence, a position which Socrates put forward, as reported in 
various Dialogues.  Plato reports that Socrates held an object had a 
quality insofar as that object reflected or partook of the Essence or Form 
(An. Gr. 'eidos') of that quality.

An action did not show 'justice' because it fit in with laws or 
situations; such things change over time and place and yet the concept of 
'just' remained constant; this was, Socratically, because there was an 
Essence, a Form, an Eidos of Justice, which existed outside of space and 
time and remained constant (as the concept of 'just' did).

Likewise with beauty... something was not beautiful because it was seen to 
be such, it was beautiful because one recognised it partook of the Eidos 
of Beauty.

(A later lover of wisdom - An. Gr. 'philosophos' - would call this 'mere 
reified concept', an attempt to make a thing - L. 'res' + L. 'faciere', 
'to make' out of something which did not otherwise exist, that it makes as 
much sense to speak of 'beauty' in the absence of things to be called 
'beautiful' as it does to speak of 'sound' in a vacuum... but what do 
these latecomers know, anyhow?)

With this concept of Forms in mind, then, it becomes readily and 
immediately apparent:

Something is humo(u)rous because it partakes of the Eidos of the Funny.

DD
```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:f922sp$d71$1@reader2.panix.com...
>
> All righty... there's been some talk 'round these parts of late as to what
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…

IMHO - all of them stumbled over the problem of definitions in every
language and situation: concepts can only be defined in terms of other
concepts - words can only be defiined in terms of other words - and sooner
or later you regress to the ultimate, totally fundamental concepts or
words - which cannot be defined.  I, Philosopher, have to accept (or hope)
that everybody knows what they mean, regardless of the lack of definitions.
Exactly analogous to mathematical axioms.  Thus Socrates speaks of the Eidos
because he can't define it but everybody will have their own understanding
of it anyway; so he evades the problem while sounding profound!

PL
```

## Thread: Ultimate efficiency using INITIALIZE statement in COBOL — 43 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Ultimate efficiency using INITIALIZE statement in COBOL

- **From:** ctolhurs@rrohio.com
- **Date:** 2007-08-04T09:17:10-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1186244230.388141.97140@i13g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`

```
I am working on developing the ultimate efficiency in terms of the
program modification and CPU usage trade-off for some Enterprise COBOL
programs that initialize large data areas with many fields.  One of
the programs has a 2500 byte area that consists of 300+ fields of
various types and sizes.  This area is re-nitialized for every
maintenance transaction that is applied, and on a recent run, there
were 55,000+ maintenance transactions.  I looked at the assembler code
generated for the initialization, and it consists of 4+ pages, at
least one for every field.  I assumed, incorrectly, that changing the
program to initialize the area once and then move it to working-
storage, and then for subsequent transactions moving the working-
storage field back had to be faster.  In reality, the compiler
generates a MVLC(length) moving 1 character at a time, and that is
very slow.  Can I call C and move more bytes at once?  If I can, will
calling C, performing the move(s) required and then returning be
faster?  If anyone has experience with this situation, please let me
know!
Thanks,
Chas
```

#### ↳ Re: Ultimate efficiency using INITIALIZE statement in COBOL

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybubNOSPAM@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2007-08-04T18:14:09-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13ba21jb67naq2d@news.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<1186244230.388141.97140@i13g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`

```
ctolhurs@rrohio.com wrote:
> I am working on developing the ultimate efficiency in terms of the
> program modification and CPU usage trade-off for some Enterprise COBOL
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> faster?  If anyone has experience with this situation, please let me

Coding for micro efficiency is usually futile. In your example, moving 2500 
bytes, one byte at a time, and assuming 6 microseconds per move, yields a 
time of 1.5 milliseconds. The logging of the resulting transaction has to 
take ten times that.

Whatever technique you use is going to take SOME time, so the savings will 
be less than the 1.5 milliseconds mentioned.

Before you get all exercised, time the existing code. Loop a million times, 
once with the initialize code and once with just the loop. Time both. Divide 
the result by 1,000,000 then multiply by 55,000. The result represents the 
maximum time saving during the day. I'll wager it's less than a couple of 
minutes.

Sometimes micro efficiency is necessary - maybe even in your situation. In 
the cases where the transaction must be processed as quickly as possible 
because other transactions are stacking up behind it, you've got to the best 
you can. Another example involves communication - again there is an urgent 
stream.

Usually, even in these two cases, it's often cheaper to just buy a faster 
machine than it is to re-program some sub-level routines.

Here's a trick. Initialize the block. Write it to a scratch file. Every time 
you need the initialized block, read it back in. Of course a good operating 
system won't actually read the disk; the system will grab the block out of a 
system buffer. Point is, the OS move may be faster than the COBOL move.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Ultimate efficiency using INITIALIZE statement in COBOL

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-08-05T11:44:16+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5hkhaaF3k3d6hU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1186244230.388141.97140@i13g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <13ba21jb67naq2d@news.supernews.com>`

```


"HeyBub" <heybubNOSPAM@gmail.com> wrote in message 
news:13ba21jb67naq2d@news.supernews.com...
> ctolhurs@rrohio.com wrote:
>> I am working on developing the ultimate efficiency in terms of the
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
> COBOL move.

Good post and good sound common sense.

I smiled at the last sentence, though... :-)

Given that the OS is running on the same platform as the application, do you 
think it gets "smarter" or faster, or special secret instructions? :-)

Ironically, there ARE "privileged" instructions on his platform, which are 
only available when the processor is in priveleged (OS, usually, unless you 
are a naughty programmer, as I sometimes was in my youth :-)) mode, but 
unfortunately, these do not include the MVC "family"...  :-)

The OS can't move stuff around any faster than COBOL (or any other language) 
does...

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ultimate efficiency using INITIALIZE statement in COBOL

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybubNOSPAM@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2007-08-04T22:41:21-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13bahmji5ub18f8@news.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<1186244230.388141.97140@i13g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <13ba21jb67naq2d@news.supernews.com> <5hkhaaF3k3d6hU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:

>
> Good post and good sound common sense.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> instructions? :-)

Yes I do. Thousands of hours are spent optimizing every jot and tittle of an 
operating system. By experts. With decades of experience. Usually. If 
there's any way to squeeze a nanosecond here or a picosecond there, they 
stand a better chance of finding it that someone who merely writes 
compilers. Now the compiler writers, on the other hand, are probably ten 
times better at extracting efficiencies (for much of the same reasons) than 
we mere mortals.

>
> Ironically, there ARE "privileged" instructions on his platform,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> "family"...  :-)

In the OP's case, the compiler generated MVC commands - storage-to-storage 
operations. Who knows but the OS may use Load/Store register commands which 
are much faster. Maybe the OS even does that kind of stuff in the channel or 
IO buss and overlap it with CPU actions. All I'm saying is that there may be 
a chance that the OS is faster than an application program if one can find 
the right trick. Even in Windows, there's a 64-bit version and a 32-bit 
version. I'll bet the 64-bit OS rendition is faster than the 32, and the 
compiler is written using 32-bit only instructions.

In my youth I worked on a big iron that had 128k of memory. We routinely (to 
the tune of several hundred reels of tape a day) read records of 300,000 
bytes, demultiplexed the records, applied gain, and reblocked, without 
invoking the CPU at all. This kind of stuff was IMPOSSIBLE to an ordinary 
application program.

>
> The OS can't move stuff around any faster than COBOL (or any other
> language) does...

Giggle. Okay, YOU write a disk defragger in COBOL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ultimate efficiency using INITIALIZE statement in COBOL

_(thread depth: 4)_

```


"HeyBub" <heybubNOSPAM@gmail.com> wrote in message 
news:13bahmji5ub18f8@news.supernews.com...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> than we mere mortals.

This is IBM...   :-)

They have a some very bright people (I know, I've worked for them :-)),  but 
we are talking about moving 2500 bytes in main storage, on an IBM 
mainframe..

It doesn't matter how clever you are, what prvileges you have, or whether 
you are having sexual relations with the CEO's wife (and his daughter...or 
his son...), you don't get to move bytes any quicker than the limitations of 
the instruction set and the hardware. :-)
>
>>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> one can find the right trick.

Yes, I know that's what you're saying :-); I'm saying it is a fallacy in the 
specific case we are talking about. I'll grant there are other operations 
where the OS has priority and smarter ways of doing things than the majority 
of application programmers would come up with. Not this one, is all.

>Even in Windows, there's a 64-bit version and a 32-bit version. I'll bet 
>the 64-bit OS rendition is faster than the 32, and the compiler is written 
>using 32-bit only instructions.

As it is irrelevant to what we are discussing, I won't take your bet :-)

>
> In my youth I worked on a big iron that had 128k of memory. We routinely 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Again you are expanding the scope of what we are talking about. (2500 bytes 
in main memory on an IBM mainframe... remember?)

While your anecdote is interesting, it can't be used to support your 
argument :-)
>>
>> The OS can't move stuff around any faster than COBOL (or any other
>> language) does...
>
> Giggle. Okay, YOU write a disk defragger in COBOL.

I wouldn't of course (I would in C#...) and I do take your point. I 
re-iterate, the rest of your post was good sound common sense so I can let 
this bit go...:-)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ultimate efficiency using INITIALIZE statement in COBOL

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybubNOSPAM@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2007-08-06T11:56:37-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13bekllnk2rk246@news.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<1186244230.388141.97140@i13g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <13ba21jb67naq2d@news.supernews.com> <5hkhaaF3k3d6hU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
>
> The OS can't move stuff around any faster than COBOL (or any other
> language) does...
>

Whether the OS can or cannot is probably immaterial. I just did a little 
test by timing a loop of a 2500-byte move. Admittedly, it was on my piddly 
PC, but 100,000,000 iterations of "MOVE A TO B" took, wait for it now.... 29 
seconds. (The PERFORM loop itself took 0.39 seconds).

That's 0.00000029 seconds per move, or, for the 55,000 transactions 
mentioned, about 16 milliseconds.

The OP, by investing several days of research, coding, and testing can 
expect a savings of, oh, 14 milliseconds per day. Max.

My original supposition: "Coding for micro efficiency is usually futile," 
still stands.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<1186244230.388141.97140@i13g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <13ba21jb67naq2d@news.supernews.com> <5hkhaaF3k3d6hU1@mid.individual.net> <1186440981.871626.293220@g4g2000hsf.googlegroups.com>`

```
Alistair wrote:
>>
>
> I was a naughty programmer once. But the sysprogs caught me hacking in
> to the system to delete a one million line output from the print queue
> (it was Sunday and they had gone home for lunch!).

Each month, I had to wait several hours while the hospital for which I 
worked printed about 12 zillion zero-balance statements.

Every dog has his day. I had to visit a doc in the emergency room for a 
minor problem. I paid cash.

In the fullness of time, I dutifully received a zero-balance statement with 
the admontion:

   "Pay this amount:   $0.00"

So I sent the hospital a check for $0.00.

Follow-up calls over succeeding months about why my check hadn't been cashed 
and did they want another were, um, educational, even inspiring.
```

Ever since you left my Nellie,
