- **Message-ID:** `<1187287125.160229.308750@i13g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<fa1ump$12j$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<1187204587.545493.218790@q4g2000prc.googlegroups.com> <1187218735.434830.115180@m37g2000prh.googlegroups.com> <46C34C79.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <1187279407.749229.171320@x35g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <fa1ump$12j$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
On Aug 16, 12:40 pm, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:
> In article <1187279407.749229.171...@x35g2000prf.googlegroups.com>,
>
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
> First reason... no END-EVALUATE for the interior EVALUATE.

OK, my brain is not a very good compiler, but you already knew
that :-). So I know this first reason is not *really* what you are
talking about, is it?

>  Second
> reason... for reasons that are readily and immediately apparent to anyone
> of good, decent, sane and sound judgement - in other words, a matter of
> Style and Aesthetics - I try to alternate IF and EVALUATE for interior
> logic.  

Ahhh, yes, the real reason - I enroached upon your easily offended
artistic sensibilities. Let me respond in my (hopefully) usual genteel
fashion...

Hmmmm... I think I have read elsewhere about the Virtue of Alternating
IF/EVALUATE; it is not an Original Idea of yours, is it? It is not a
matter that brings forth strong feelings on my part. I could certainly
adhere to any such directive; I could certainly adhere to one without
it :-).

But rather than digress into that matter, let me emphasize what may be
the not-too-obvious. You and I here are now discussing what is under
only _one leg_ of the outer-EVALUATE. And the leg in question has as
its given a condition of CASE-EXISTS-EXACTLY. This is of itself a
significant step forward from where we were before.

After all, all of the code you challenged could now be encapsulated
into its own paragraph, and the exterior becomes...

    EVALUATE TRUE
      WHEN  CASE-IN-COMPLETE
        SET   IN-COMPLETE-CASE  TO TRUE
        PERFORM 1000-SET-CASE-TYPE-MSG
      WHEN  NO-CASE-EXISTS
        SET  NON-EXISTING-CASE  TO TRUE
        PERFORM 1000-SET-CASE-TYPE-MSG
      WHEN  CASE-EXISTS-BUT-NOT-EXACT
        SET  NON-MATCHING-CASE  TO TRUE
        PERFORM C110-SET-CASE-TYPE-MSG
      WHEN CASE-EXISTS-EXACTLY
* *** NOW PROCESS VERY GOOD CASE ACCORDING TO ITS STATUS...
        PERFORM 2000-PROCESS-EXACT-CASE
      WHEN OTHER
        DISPLAY "BAD INTERNAL ERROR IN " WS-CURRENT-PARAGRAPH
        PERFORM 999-ABEND-EXIT
    END-EVALUATE

See, now we need only discuss what 2000-PROCESS-EXACT-CASE should look
like. We have now scoped and bounded the problem area a bit.
As I have said (indirectly) to the client - you have your choice - I
can rewrite this subsystem all at once, or one defect fix at a time.
My goals are modest - just make it a little bit better a fix at a
time.

>
> (by the bye.. this code does not give the same results when
> (IND-TYPE-OF-CASE = SPACES AND CASE-OPEN OF WEA1CAS-CASE-STATUS),
> either... but That Could Never Happen, right?
>

Ummmmm, yes indeed, this is some of the perils of the Booleans in the
condition. And under what circumstances is the WHEN OTHER satisfied?
Will Mr. Dashwood please step forward to apply his De Morgan's
Laws? :-)

What was it you said earlier? Ahhh, yes, here it is...

"What should be considered the case when using a 'case' construct,
then, seems to be worthy of more than passing examination... LEFT-
HANDED does not rule out BROWN-EYED does not rule out HAS-SIX-TOES."

I believe that you are right on target here. To do such a "more than
passing examination" does not seem to be a merely pedantic exercise to
me. Rather, I think it gets right to the Heart of the Matter.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Regarding EVALUATE TRUE

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-08-17T18:06:51+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5iks7rF3obe7oU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1187204587.545493.218790@q4g2000prc.googlegroups.com> <1187218735.434830.115180@m37g2000prh.googlegroups.com> <46C34C79.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <1187279407.749229.171320@x35g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <fa1ump$12j$1@reader1.panix.com> <1187287125.160229.308750@i13g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`

```


<klshafer@att.net> wrote in message 
news:1187287125.160229.308750@i13g2000prf.googlegroups.com...
> On Aug 16, 12:40 pm, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:
>> In article <1187279407.749229.171...@x35g2000prf.googlegroups.com>,
…[104 more quoted lines elided]…
> Laws? :-)

Well, they're not MY laws, and, actually having a life that no longer 
involves pedantic arguments about the infinitely stupid ways in which people 
can and do write COBOL, you will understand my staying at the back of the 
class on this, avoiding Teacher's gaze, just keeping one eye on it and one 
ear half open, in the forlorn hope that there might be something amusing or 
entertaining,  and applying what feeble few brain cells I have left to 
ensuring that "enterprises of great pitch and moment" do not, "with this 
regard" have their "currents turn awry, and lose the name of action."

In other words, "I'm busy...", but, some here think that  saying that (even 
when it's true) is just a Management excuse for not doing something, and 
being the insecure shrinking violet that I am, in desperate need of the 
approval of CLC, I took a few moments to cast an eye over the redundant mess 
you posted above.

The first thing that struck me is that it tests a condition and then sets 
another condition...

WHEN  CASE-IN-COMPLETE
             SET   IN-COMPLETE-CASE  TO TRUE

... and it does this on several occasions.

I suppose if you have a vested interest in hastening the heat death of the 
Universe, this might be acceptable, but to me it is just nonsense. Normally, 
I wouldn't waste time on it, (and would charge a serious sum of money if I 
did) but since you asked,  and since I have spent most of the morning 
engrossed in real work and am in need of some light relief, (and I can't 
find a cryptic crossword to refresh my thought processes), I have spent the 
time instead, applying good old George's postulates and  Augustus De 
Morgan's extensions of them, to see if the yarn can be unravelled...

I believe the following valid COBOL could replace the EVALUATE above without 
any loss of functionality:

IF NOT CASE-EXISTS-EXACTLY

     IF NOT  CASE-EXISTS-BUT-NOT-EXACT

         PERFORM 1000-SET-CASE-TYPE-MSG

     ELSE

         PERFORM C110-SET-CASE-TYPE-MSG

     END-IF

ELSE

      IF NOT WEA1CAS-STATUS   =  IND-TYPE-OF-CASE

              EVALUATE TRUE

                     WHEN  WEA1CAS-STATUS   >  SPACE  AND  CASE-OPEN OF 
WEA1CAS-SACSTAT

                                        PERFORM 2000-FINISH-CASE-EDIT-S

                      WHEN  (WEA1CAS-STATUS   >  SPACE AND CASE-CLOSED OF 
WEA1CAS-CASE-STATUS)  OR

                                    (WEA1CAS-STATUS   =  SPACE AND CASE-OPEN 
OF WEA1CAS-CASE-STATUS AND

                                    CASE--OPEN OF CSN-CASE-STATUS)

                                         PERFORM 3000-FORMAT-WEA1CASA
                                         PERFORM 4000-UPDATE-WEA1CASA-6CASH

               END-EVALUATE

       END-IF

END-IF



It is  around half of the original code.



It has had redundant tests and actions removed, does not need CONTINUE or 
WHEN OTHER because these have been percolated to the bottom so they simply 
fall off.



It does make the following assumptions:



1. There's no way I will code the setting of a flag based on a flag, so you 
must consider the following to be logically equivalent:



        CASE-IN-COMPLETE = IN-COMPLETE-CASE

        NO-CASE-EXISTS      = NON-EXISTING-CASE

        CASE-EXISTS-BUT-NOT-EXACT = NON-MATCHING-CASE



(If it is a flag in a different record, then set ALL the flags in the record 
immediately before you write it, using the single instance master flags in 
WS. If it's NOT in a different record then do a global edit and replace 
every reference to the right side of the equivaleneces above, replacing them 
with the left side.Then remove the stupid SETs.).



2.  The solution assumes that the Universe is what was posted. For example, 
there are no other unreferenced 88s on the same flag as the ones described. 
It is easy to amend the underlying Boolean truth table if there are, but for 
this exercise, no...



3. I have not read previous posts in this thread (which might have different 
code or discussion, or clarify or obfuscate the code above.) and the 
solution is limited only to what is here. (See point 2, above).



No warranty is expressed or implied; I did this Simplification in my "tea 
break" as a welcome relief from design logic problems. If it is wrong I'm 
embarrassed and sorry, but don't expect any money :-) It has not been 
subject to the QA that work which I normally do is put through.



As for your question, well, it is now pointless because there is no need for 
WHEN OTHER. . However, for the sake of completeness, it would be the 
negation of ALL of the conditions in ALL of the WHENs above it...



WHEN OTHER =  IF NOT (all conditions in WHENs, parenthesized and ORed )




> What was it you said earlier? Ahhh, yes, here it is...
>
> "What should be considered the case when using a 'case' construct,
> then, seems to be worthy of more than passing examination... LEFT-
> HANDED does not rule out BROWN-EYED does not rule out HAS-SIX-TOES."



Er...it DOES sometimes....



01 SILLY-FLAG   PIC X VALUE SPACE.

     88 LEFT-HANDED     VALUE '1'.

     88 BROWN-EYED     VALUE '2'.

     88 HAS-SIX-TOES     VALUE '3'.



>
> I believe that you are right on target here. To do such a "more than
> passing examination" does not seem to be a merely pedantic exercise to
> me. Rather, I think it gets right to the Heart of the Matter.



Only as long as you realise that thinking in English is NOT the same as 
thinking in COBOL...



Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Regarding EVALUATE TRUE

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "klshafer@att.net" <klshafer@att.net>
- **Date:** 2007-08-17T07:54:56-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1187362496.040834.44620@i13g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<5iks7rF3obe7oU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1187204587.545493.218790@q4g2000prc.googlegroups.com> <1187218735.434830.115180@m37g2000prh.googlegroups.com> <46C34C79.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <1187279407.749229.171320@x35g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <fa1ump$12j$1@reader1.panix.com> <1187287125.160229.308750@i13g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <5iks7rF3obe7oU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
Original code as >> ;
Mr. Dashwood's comments as >
>>    EVALUATE TRUE
>>      WHEN  CASE-IN-COMPLETE
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Hmmmm.... thank you for taking time out of your busy day, Mr.
Dashwood, because your observation above has been helpful. It piqued
my curiosity to ask, now just _why_ did the original developer use the
value of a flag just to set another flag? Ahhhh, this is why:
         10  MSG-ENTRY-012.
             15  FILLER          PIC X(05)   VALUE
 
'CASE'.                                                   15  MSG-12-
ACCOUNT-ID
                                 PIC X(10).
             15  FILLER          PIC X(10)   VALUE
                       ', W/OWNER'.
             15  MSG-12-OWNER-ID PIC X(05).
             15  FILLER          PIC X(18).
               88  CASE-CREATED-MSG          VALUE
                       ', IS NOW CREATED'.
               88    IN-COMPLETE-CASE        VALUE
                       ', NOT COMPLETE'.
               88   NON-MATCHING-CASE        VALUE
                       ', NON-MATCHING ID'.
               88   NON-EXISTING-CASE        VALUE
                       ', IS NOT ON FILE'.
               88    IN-VALID-TYPE-CASE      VALUE
                       ', NOT VALID TYPE'.

Oooooooohhhhh - look at this, IN-COMPLETE-CASE is not really an
"indicator flag" - it is a text portion of a journal message!
Setting the 88 has the effect of setting the 15-level FILLER to that
value. Now using an 88-level to set a value for FILLER is not an idea
that has ever come to me - so one learns something new every day!

On the other hand, I think what I learned is yet another way that a
programmer "can be too clever for his own good", or more accurately, a
programmer "can be too clever for the good of the maintainers that
follow...".

Perhaps I could be more accepting of the trickery above if all of
these 88-levels had been named something like the first one, namely
CASE-CREATED-MSG had been followed in spirit by IN-COMPLETE-CASE-MSG,
NON-MATCHING-CASE-MSG, and the like. This would provided a clue that
what was being set was a message portion, and not a flag.

However, such tentative acceptance of this trickery might be more
charitable on my part toward the original developer than he really
deserves. A quick browse of the code shows that the fragment above is
the ONLY place where those error message fragments are set - only one
occurrence per 88-level.

Rather than using FILLER, the intent of the code would be so much more
clear by using a more traditional naming like MSG-12-INFO-TEXT,
leaving out the message 88's altogether, and changing the SET
statements above to the more forthright MOVE ", NOT COMPLETE" TO
MSG-12-INFO-TEXT. The result would be less lines of code (in keeping
with your preference for parsimony, Mr. Dashwood), and a more direct
approach (in keeping with my preference for understandability over
cleverness.)

Thanks,
Ken
```

```
<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:fa4eev$d9i$1@reader1.panix.com...
```

- **References:** `<1187204587.545493.218790@q4g2000prc.googlegroups.com> <1187218735.434830.115180@m37g2000prh.googlegroups.com> <46C34C79.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <1187279407.749229.171320@x35g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <fa1ump$12j$1@reader1.panix.com> <1187287125.160229.308750@i13g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <5iks7rF3obe7oU1@mid.individual.net> <1187362496.040834.44620@i13g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`

```
<klshafer@att.net> wrote in message 
news:1187362496.040834.44620@i13g2000prf.googlegroups.com...
> Original code as >> ;
>>

I was a little disappointed that, in response to your request, and having 
taken the (admittedly short, and for amusement only) time to apply Boolean 
simplification to:

     EVALUATE TRUE
       WHEN  CASE-IN-COMPLETE
         SET   IN-COMPLETE-CASE  TO TRUE
         PERFORM 1000-SET-CASE-TYPE-MSG
       WHEN  NO-CASE-EXISTS
         SET  NON-EXISTING-CASE  TO TRUE
         PERFORM 1000-SET-CASE-TYPE-MSG
       WHEN  CASE-EXISTS-BUT-NOT-EXACT
         SET  NON-MATCHING-CASE  TO TRUE
         PERFORM C110-SET-CASE-TYPE-MSG
       WHEN CASE-EXISTS-EXACTLY
* *** NOW PROCESS VERY GOOD CASE ACCORDING TO ITS STATUS...
         EVALUATE TRUE
           WHEN   WEA1CAS-STATUS   =  IND-TYPE-OF-CASE
             CONTINUE
           WHEN   WEA1CAS-STATUS   >  SPACE
            AND   CASE-OPEN       OF WEA1CAS-SACSTAT
             PERFORM 2000-FINISH-CASE-EDIT-S
           WHEN   WEA1CAS-STATUS   >  SPACE
            AND   CASE-CLOSED     OF WEA1CAS-CASE-STATUS
             PERFORM 3000-FORMAT-WEA1CASA
            PERFORM 4000-UPDATE-WEA1CASA-6CASH
           WHEN   WEA1CAS-STATUS   =  SPACE
            AND   CASE-OPEN       OF WEA1CAS-CASE-STATUS
              IF CASE--OPEN OF CSN-CASE-STATUS
                 PERFORM 3000-FORMAT-WEA1CASA
                 PERFORM 4000-UPDATE-WEA1CASA-6CASH
               ELSE
                 CONTINUE
              END-IF
           WHEN OTHER
             CONTINUE
       WHEN OTHER
         DISPLAY "BAD INTERNAL ERROR - SHOULD NOT REACH HERE!"
         PERFORM 999-ABEND-EXIT
     END-EVALUATE

and obtained...

IF NOT CASE-EXISTS-EXACTLY
     IF NOT  CASE-EXISTS-BUT-NOT-EXACT
         PERFORM 1000-SET-CASE-TYPE-MSG
     ELSE
         PERFORM C110-SET-CASE-TYPE-MSG
     END-IF
ELSE
      IF NOT WEA1CAS-STATUS   =  IND-TYPE-OF-CASE
              EVALUATE TRUE
                    WHEN  WEA1CAS-STATUS   >  SPACE  AND
                           CASE-OPEN OF WEA1CAS-SACSTAT
                                 PERFORM 2000-FINISH-CASE-EDIT-S
                     WHEN   (WEA1CAS-STATUS   >  SPACE AND
                           CASE-CLOSED OF WEA1CAS-CASE-STATUS)  OR
                           (WEA1CAS-STATUS   =  SPACE AND
                            CASE-OPEN OF WEA1CAS-CASE-STATUS AND
                            CASE--OPEN OF CSN-CASE-STATUS)
                                 PERFORM 3000-FORMAT-WEA1CASA
                                 PERFORM 4000-UPDATE-WEA1CASA-6CASH
               END-EVALUATE
       END-IF
END-IF

... you then chose to ignore this result completely and snipped it from your 
response.

I think this is pretty amazing. Especially when you think it was postulated 
by two people who lived more than 100 years before the first electronic 
computer was ever assembled. (there is apochryphal evidence that Augustus De 
Morgan assisted Lady Lovelace to write the first "program" for Babbage's 
ill-fated Analytical Engine, which might very well have been the first 
computer program ever written.)

(Alistair might be interested to know that De Morgan lost the sight of his 
right eye soon after birth and was teased at school about it.)

Given that you claimed in an earlier post to be "cleaning up" the mess, I 
would have thought this would be of some relevance.

Instead, it seems you were distracted by the shiny bauble of a coding 
technique you hadn't seen before :-)

>> The first thing that struck me is that it tests a condition and then sets
>> another condition...
…[52 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Perhaps optimization of convoluted code might be on the agenda some time in 
the future?

> On the other hand, I think what I learned is yet another way that a
> programmer "can be too clever for his own good", or more accurately, a
> programmer "can be too clever for the good of the maintainers that
> follow...".

So much for style.

Now, how about a programmer actually cleaning up bad code?  Your thoughts on 
a postage stamp, by return, please :-)

<snip>

> The result would be less lines of code (in keeping
> with your preference for parsimony, Mr. Dashwood), and a more direct
> approach (in keeping with my preference for understandability over
> cleverness.)
>

I have found in my (admittedly still ongoing) experience that parsimony 
often helps understandability.

I seem to recall something about brevity being the soul of wit... it is also 
the soul of COBOL  maintenance programming. The less there is, the more 
quickly it can be assimilated.

Pete.
```

```
NonStop product line or its support staff, all of which and most of whom UB 
inherited from Tandem via Compaq, and are almost uniformly top-notch. Of 
course, it was already that way when they bought it, and the clowns at Useless 
Bastard deserve none of the credit.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<1187204587.545493.218790@q4g2000prc.googlegroups.com> <1187218735.434830.115180@m37g2000prh.googlegroups.com> <46C34C79.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <1187279407.749229.171320@x35g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <fa1ump$12j$1@reader1.panix.com> <1187287125.160229.308750@i13g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <5iks7rF3obe7oU1@mid.individual.net> <1187362496.040834.44620@i13g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <5in0hdF3qif6mU1@mid.individual.net> <1187433035.501934.211450@r34g2000hsd.googlegroups.com> <fac59o$lfb$2@usenet01.boi.hp.com> <5j1nhhF3ptgukU1@mid.individual.net> <13cod9uk7rqr73c@news.supernews.com> <vUWyi.4463$LL7.2424@nlpi069.nbdc.sbc.com> <5j322rF3s4rr9U1@mid.individual.net> <S9Zyi.34455$2v1.11590@newssvr14.news.prodigy.net>`

```


"Doug Miller" <spambait@milmac.com> wrote in message 
news:S9Zyi.34455$2v1.11590@newssvr14.news.prodigy.net...
> In article <5j322rF3s4rr9U1@mid.individual.net>, "Pete Dashwood" 
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bastard deserve none of the credit.

Fair enough. :-)

Sadly, they are not the only company to arouse this kind of ire and I've 
experienced a few "useless bastards" myself... :-)

Fortunately (for me, at least) I haven't encountered it with HP, but I have 
never dealt with their US support.

I guess it is down to all of us, as software professionals to try and see 
that what we do  is unlikely to lead to the "unpleasant experience" with 
support that you describe above.

That's why I think that Quality Control is an indispensable part of the 
software development cycle, and also why I am a big fan of investing time 
"up front" into design.

I like to use the RAD approach (interactive and iterative) where the end 
users are part of the design process (joint workshops are good...) and are 
engaged right through the cycle. The other spinoff from this is that 
development is prioritised and timeboxed so the problem which led John to 
post in the first place (not enough time to think about improving things 
with pressure to get it done, and consequently, not touching things that 
already work) simply doesn't arise. Instead, the users know that when the 
timebox is up there is no slippage or extensions; they have to get their 
priorities right and make sure that what they "really, really, want" (sorry 
Spice Girls :-)) is a "must have". Also, this approach encorages the 
revisiting of code after it has been in use. End users can feed back what 
they would like to see changed or ways of improving it, and this is fed into 
the cycle and prioritised, just as any other requirement.

Users should never feel they are having systems "thrust upon them" by IT; it 
is much more beneficial for them, IT and the Company as a whole when they 
are involved throughout and feel that the system is as much theirs as it is 
IT's.

I do have sympathy for John's POV because I know that many companies put 
huge pressure on development teams to get "something" as soon as possible. 
(Another spinoff from RAD is that functions can be "trickled out" as they 
are developed and agreed, rather than waiting for the "Big Bang" on delivery 
day.)

At a programming level I'm sure many here have seen the case where a guy 
takes a spec. and sits down for some time before starting to code. 
Short-sighted Managers think he is "doing nothing" while his colleague has 
ripped into it and is in the final stages of testing by the time our man 
starts to code. It isn't hard to know whose code is going to be less 
problematic for the future, and whose is going to make people swear :-). 
Personally, I'm happy for the development of a given program to take an 
extra day or so if the quality is better and the design is sound.

For me, "Better is NOT the enemy of Done, Better is why we come to work..."

Pete
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Regarding EVALUATE TRUE

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-08-23T02:56:30+12:00

```
made it difficult to read. Now that you have presented a well-
formatted version, I'll spend time on it...
>

Yes, sometimes simple tools are powerful.

> Given that you claimed in an earlier post to be "cleaning up" the mess, I

Yes, it is relevant.

>

Well, I picked the "low hanging fruit" first from your help. The part
about how the "flag" wasn't really a flag was significant, it was
easier, it helped, and it was consistent with the effort of
understanding the outer EVALUATE first.

> Perhaps optimization of convoluted code might be on the agenda some time in

Yes, perhaps it might be. After sufficient understanding is reached.
Please let me explain. Let's review some context of what I said -

"And under what circumstances is the WHEN OTHER satisfied?
Will Mr. Dashwood please step forward to apply his De Morgan's
Laws? :-)"

Some of the defects are manifesting when this code segment doese
program does the "drop through", i.e., executes the "do nothing" WHEN
OTHER ... CONTINUE in the original, or simply "executes nothing
gracefully", as in your amended version. This is what I had in mind
when I also said earler, "It is left as an exercise for all the
Students reading this forum to determine the conditions under which
the WHEN OTHER is executed :-)."

Mr. Dashwood, I do believe that it will be easier for me to obtain
this answer by negating the AND's in your condensed version than it
would be for me to operate on the original EVALUATE. GThis is helpful
to me.

The problem here is that the program is sometimes (improperly)
dropping through, doing nothing, and not doing something that it
should. This is equivalent to "all the other decision criteria" not
being entirely correct. The spec doesn't say (there isn't any), and
the users can't tell me what those conditions are. We are definitely
pulling ourselves up by our bootstraps here.

Ken
```

```
>> I was a little disappointed that, in response to your request, and having

…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> formatted version, I'll spend time on it...

Ah, I was interested to see what would happen with that. I'm sorry, the 
original post didn't format well because I copied it from a word document, 
where I built the Boolean truth table and simplification.

It was only after it was posted that I saw how awful it looked and 
reformatted it.

The interesting point is that when we encounter things that don't look 
familiar, we tend to ignore them. If the posted (badly formatted) code had 
been cut and pasted into a COBOL editor, it would have resolved into the 
same format as the second post.

This tends to confirm something that Richard has been saying here for years; 
what we are used to is what we feel is good.

I appreciate your honesty. Thanks.

>>
>> I think this is pretty amazing. Especially when you think it was 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Yes, it is relevant.

OK, that's good :-)

>
>>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> understanding the outer EVALUATE first.

I understand. Obviously, for me, my time was devoted to a Boolean 
simplification of the original convoluted code, so I was interested to see 
your (and possibly others) response to that. I don't think people realise 
how much can be gained by investing a little time in understanding the rules 
of Boolean Algebra (not just De Morgan's, but the whole thing.) Anyone who 
enjoys logic problems (and that is most programmers, in my experience) 
should take to it, like ducks to water.

It is actually a lot of fun :-).

For example, here's a problem I formulated 30 years ago for a quick seminar 
on Boolean Algebra and propositional calculus:

Four men are accused of a crime.

Abe said: "Bert did it".
Bert said "Dan did it".
Charlie said: "I didn't do it".
Dan said: " Bert's lying when he says I did it."

Unfortunately, only one of them is telling the truth. :-)

Who is the honest man, and who is the culprit?

At first glance, it looks complex, yet in fact, it is remarkably simple.

And the same laws that can cut a three page nested IF in COBOL down to less 
than 30 lines (actual case...on a site in Spain),  can also reveal who is 
honest and who isn't.
>
>> Perhaps optimization of convoluted code might be on the agenda some time 
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> to me.

Good. :-)

Or, you could substitute it as written and use CONTINUE :-) (That was NOT 
serious...:-))

>
> The problem here is that the program is sometimes (improperly)
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You have my sympathy. Been there... :-)
>
>> > On the other hand, I think what I learned is yet another way that a
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well the comment was intended as a jest at you being sidetracked. It wasn't 
meant to wound, and to be fair, you weren't sidetracked from what was of 
interest to YOU. I was slightly miffed at my time being wasted :-)

Just a note in passing...

As I never attended an American College, I find the use of the formal "Mr" 
to be unfriendly and cold. (It brings out the worst in me and leads to a 
cold response, or none at all.). A bit like being addressed as "Sir" when 
you are an NCO... I think the technical term is "exalting".

If people wish to communicate with me here, please, use my name. Adopting 
the formal, indicates displeasure or hostility as far as I am concerned.

The only exception is Doc Dwarf and I cut him slack because he is the King 
of England, and because we both go back around a decade in this group. 
(Besides, his posts to me are cold and unfriendly no matter what form of 
address he chooses... :-))

BOTTOM LINE: If you want my feedback or thoughts, address me as Pete. If you 
are pissed off with something I wrote, and don't want a friendly discussion 
about it, address me as Mr. (and you may or may not get a response). If I am 
really pissed off with something you wrote, you'll know...:-)

>
>> <snip>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> exhaustive coverage helps me more.

The two are not mutually exclusive and, in fact, one may be very conducive 
to the other. It is easier to completely cover a small amount of code than 
it is a large amount.
>
> Thanks for taking time to help, Mr. Dashwood... I appreciate it.

If something was gained, it is time well spent.

Pete.
```

```
In article <CRRyi.451070$p47.317998@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>, arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net wrote:
>Pete Dashwood wrote:
>> (snip)
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>developed an answer, I believe Charlie is the miscreant.

And Dan is the honest man.

My reasoning: exactly one of the men is telling the truth. Since Bert and Dan 
directly contradict each other, they can't *both* be lying, so the honest man 
is one or the other of that pair. That means Abe and Charlie are both liars, 
which in turn means that Charlie's profession of innocence is a lie. And Dan 
must be telling the truth when he says Bert's lying.

Now suppose only one of the men is *lying*, and the other three are telling 
the truth. Who's the culprit this time?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Regarding EVALUATE TRUE

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-08-23T02:41:40+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5j3096F3rttstU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1187204587.545493.218790@q4g2000prc.googlegroups.com> <1187218735.434830.115180@m37g2000prh.googlegroups.com> <46C34C79.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <1187279407.749229.171320@x35g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <fa1ump$12j$1@reader1.panix.com> <1187287125.160229.308750@i13g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <5iks7rF3obe7oU1@mid.individual.net> <1187362496.040834.44620@i13g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <5in0hdF3qif6mU1@mid.individual.net> <1187723493.834715.99700@e9g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <5j1ma0F3s1j96U1@mid.individual.net> <CRRyi.451070$p47.317998@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <0EUyi.750$YQ.343@nlpi061.nbdc.sbc.com>`

```
"Doug Miller" <spambait@milmac.com> wrote in message 
news:0EUyi.750$YQ.343@nlpi061.nbdc.sbc.com...
> In article <CRRyi.451070$p47.317998@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>, 
> arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net wrote:
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> must be telling the truth when he says Bert's lying.

Good job, Doug. Quite flawless...:-)

For the benefit of those who want to know how to resolve such things,the 
answer is, of course, to prepare a "truth table".

  statement             A            B            C            D
       Abe                0            1              0            0
       Bert                0            0              0            1
      Charlie             1            1              0            1
       Dan                1            1              1            0

The table above uses 1 to represent TRUE and 0 to represent FALSE

Looking at the rows, we see the statements made by each of them. Note that 
Charlie's "I didn't do it" means that one of the others must have (In effect 
he is accusing three of them). Dan's statement we can see immediately is the 
negation of Bert's statement.

Now consider the fact that only ONE of them is telling the truth.

This means that one COLUMN must have a single true value in it.

We find the requisite column is the one for Charlie, so he must be the 
miscreant, and it is Dan's row that contains the single true value, so he 
must be telling the truth.


> Now suppose only one of the men is *lying*, and the other three are 
> telling
> the truth. Who's the culprit this time?
>

An interesting question...

Lets re-interpret the truth table...

  statement             A            B            C            D
       Abe                0            1              0            0
       Bert                0            0              0            1
      Charlie             1            1              0            1
       Dan                1            1              1            0

If three of them are telling the truth, we need a column containing 3 true 
values. The only column that qualifies is Bert's. As Bert's is also the only 
row that has a zero in the B(ert) column, we can safely conclude that Bert 
is a criminal and a liar... :-)


Now back to COBOL...

To unravel convoluted logic, we need to decide what conditions cause what 
actions.

Here is Ken's EVALUATE:

      EVALUATE TRUE
       WHEN  CASE-IN-COMPLETE
         SET   IN-COMPLETE-CASE  TO TRUE
         PERFORM 1000-SET-CASE-TYPE-MSG
       WHEN  NO-CASE-EXISTS
         SET  NON-EXISTING-CASE  TO TRUE
         PERFORM 1000-SET-CASE-TYPE-MSG
       WHEN  CASE-EXISTS-BUT-NOT-EXACT
         SET  NON-MATCHING-CASE  TO TRUE
         PERFORM C110-SET-CASE-TYPE-MSG
       WHEN CASE-EXISTS-EXACTLY
* *** NOW PROCESS VERY GOOD CASE ACCORDING TO ITS STATUS...
         EVALUATE TRUE
           WHEN   WEA1CAS-STATUS   =  IND-TYPE-OF-CASE
             CONTINUE
           WHEN   WEA1CAS-STATUS   >  SPACE
            AND   CASE-OPEN       OF WEA1CAS-SACSTAT
             PERFORM 2000-FINISH-CASE-EDIT-S
           WHEN   WEA1CAS-STATUS   >  SPACE
            AND   CASE-CLOSED     OF WEA1CAS-CASE-STATUS
             PERFORM 3000-FORMAT-WEA1CASA
            PERFORM 4000-UPDATE-WEA1CASA-6CASH
           WHEN   WEA1CAS-STATUS   =  SPACE
            AND   CASE-OPEN       OF WEA1CAS-CASE-STATUS
              IF CASE--OPEN OF CSN-CASE-STATUS
                 PERFORM 3000-FORMAT-WEA1CASA
                 PERFORM 4000-UPDATE-WEA1CASA-6CASH
               ELSE
                 CONTINUE
              END-IF
           WHEN OTHER
             CONTINUE
       WHEN OTHER
         DISPLAY "BAD INTERNAL ERROR - SHOULD NOT REACH HERE!"
         PERFORM 999-ABEND-EXIT
     END-EVALUATE

Start by isolating all the conditions and all the actions, and assigning an 
identifier to them. I have used letters for the conditions and numbers for 
the actions. Note that a condition or an action is never repeated in the 
table; entries must be unique. Also, if a condition is negated, it does NOT 
get another value assigned to the negated form. ONE entry only for each 
condition...

Here is the truth table for Ken's EVALUATE...


CONDITIONS
----------------------------------------------------
A CASE-IN-COMPLETE
B NO-CASE-EXISTS
C CASE-EXISTS-BUT-NOT-EXACT
D CASE-EXISTS-EXACTLY
E WEA1CAS-STATUS = IND-TYPE-OF-CASE
F WEA1CAS-STATUS   >  SPACE
G CASE-OPEN OF WEA1CAS-SACSTAT
H CASE-CLOSED OF WEA1CAS-CASE-STATUS
J WEA1CAS-STATUS   =  SPACE
K CASE-OPEN  OF WEA1CAS-CASE-STATUS
L CASE--OPEN OF CSN-CASE-STATUS

ACTIONS
-------------------------------------------------------
1 PERFORM 1000-SET-CASE-TYPE-MSG
2 PERFORM C110-SET-CASE-TYPE-MSG
3 PERFORM 2000-FINISH-CASE-EDIT-S
4 PERFORM 3000-FORMAT-WEA1CASA
   PERFORM 4000-UPDATE-WEA1CASA-6CASH

We see immediately there are only 4 actions required. Now lets formulate a 
Boolean proposition for each of the actions...

1 =  A + B  [Action 1 is performed if A or B is true]
2 =  C  -> (A + B)'  [Action 2 is performed if and only if C is True. C 
implies NOT (A OR B)...
        A + B + C -> D' [A OR B OR C implies NOT D]
3 =  D.E'.F.G  [Action 3 is performed if   D AND NOT E AND F AND G]
4 =  D.E'.F.H  + D.E'.J.K.L [Action 4 is performed if (D AND NOT E AND F AND 
H) OR (D AND NOT E AND J AND K AND L)]

Simplifying...(to do this properly you need a copy of the Laws of a Boolean 
Algebra, which includes De Morgan 1 and 2. These Laws are not complex and 
there aren't too many of them. Here's a site that you can download them 
from: 
http://www.ee.ic.ac.uk/pcheung/teaching/ee1_digital/Lecture4-Boolean%20Algebra.pdf )

3 = D.E'(F.G)               [Associative Law]
4 = D.E'(F.H + J.K.L)  [Associative Law]

Now formulate COBOL...

A + B + C implies D' so test that first

If NOT D           [it's A + B + C]

    IF NOT  C     [it's A + B]

         Then 1

    Else                [it's C]

         Then  2

    END-IF

ELSE                  [it's D...check  E]

     IF NOT E      [D.E'... common requirement for actions 3 and 4]

          EVALUATE TRUE

                 WHEN  F AND G

                          Then 3

                  WHEN  (F AND H)  OR (J AND K AND L)

                          Then 4

          END-EVALUATE

      END-IF

END-IF



Substituting for symbols in the above construct...


IF NOT CASE-EXISTS-EXACTLY
     IF NOT  CASE-EXISTS-BUT-NOT-EXACT
         PERFORM 1000-SET-CASE-TYPE-MSG
     ELSE
         PERFORM C110-SET-CASE-TYPE-MSG
     END-IF
ELSE
      IF NOT WEA1CAS-STATUS   =  IND-TYPE-OF-CASE
              EVALUATE TRUE
                     WHEN  WEA1CAS-STATUS   >  SPACE  AND
                           CASE-OPEN OF WEA1CAS-SACSTAT
                             PERFORM 2000-FINISH-CASE-EDIT-S
                    WHEN   (WEA1CAS-STATUS   >  SPACE AND
                           CASE-CLOSED OF WEA1CAS-CASE-STATUS)  OR
                           (WEA1CAS-STATUS   =  SPACE AND
                            CASE-OPEN OF WEA1CAS-CASE-STATUS AND
                            CASE--OPEN OF CSN-CASE-STATUS)
                             PERFORM 3000-FORMAT-WEA1CASA
                             PERFORM 4000-UPDATE-WEA1CASA-6CASH
               END-EVALUATE
       END-IF
END-IF

Done!

I hope the above has shown that Boolean Logic and propositional calculus can 
not only be useful, but fun too. This particular instance had few negated 
conditions (it's a lot more fun when they do, and the results are much more 
spectacular :-))

Now, let's not hear any more talk about removing NOT from COBOL, please :-) 
(as you can see, it is part of a set... :-))

And could we stop setting 88 levels to YES and NO :-)? (there are axioms of 
the Algebra that rely on 1 being true and 0 being false; e.g. A . 1 is 
always TRUE; A . 0 is always FALSE, just as a couple of starters...

(1 = TRUE;  0 = FALSE   It's much more professional and doesn't upset people 
in non-English speaking countries who have to maintain code from head 
office. I have worked on such sites and heard the comments... :-)) Also, 
don't set the intial value to 0. It should be indeterminate until the flag 
is used. One day you may work in an environment where your code has multiple 
copies running in real time, some of which could even be shared. Don't rely 
on flags being initialized.

01 boolean-flag pic x value space.
     88  flag-is-set                  value '1'.
     88  not-flag-is-set.           value '0'.

Yeah, I know... it's all about style, and what you're used to... :-)

Nevertheless, spare a thought for George and Augustus, if not for poor old 
Pete who has laboured to give you this...:-)

Pete
```

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2007-08-22T09:27:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fagvie$fhm$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<1187204587.545493.218790@q4g2000prc.googlegroups.com> <5in0hdF3qif6mU1@mid.individual.net> <1187723493.834715.99700@e9g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <5j1ma0F3s1j96U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
In article <5j1ma0F3s1j96U1@mid.individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

[snip]

>Just a note in passing...
>
>As I never attended an American College, I find the use of the formal "Mr" 
>to be unfriendly and cold.

In my experience, Mr Dashwood, the use of 'Mr' is not common to American 
college campuses; my alma mater was considered a bit... odd because the 
term was used universally.  A professor (called a 'tutor', for some 
reason), would not say 'Now Jimmy over there has a good point'; the 
students were Mr Jones and Ms Smith *just* as the faculty, from President 
to Junior Lab Assistant, were Mr Green and Ms Blue... just as the 
groundskeepers, cooks and serving-folks in the cafeteria were Mr Carpenter 
and Ms Taylor... or at least so the food tasted.

At any rate, the point of the formality was that each person deserved it 
and was expected to get it; rather than 'exalting' (if that it the word) 
it was... democratising?  egalitising?  equally saucy, for gooses and 
ganders.

[snip]

>The only exception is Doc Dwarf and I cut him slack because he is the King 
>of England, and because we both go back around a decade in this group. 
>(Besides, his posts to me are cold and unfriendly no matter what form of 
>address he chooses... :-))

You might learn something from one of my tutors... errrrrr, professors, Mr 
Dashwood... then again, so might I.  One fellow with whom I had a class 
(classes were almost entirely discussion, not lecture) every year but my 
Junior looked at me, during a Senior-year class as I was... gently 
correcting a classmate's view and said 'You know... I've suddenly realised 
that everything you do is... *tinged* with goodwill.'

I smiled and supplied the requisite 'Thank you, Mr Smythe'... while 
inwardly thinking 'Dear Lord... this man is a College Professor and it 
took him three years' worth of almost thrice-weekly contact to discover 
this?'

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Regarding EVALUATE TRUE

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-08-23T02:48:37+12:00

- **Message-ID:** `<1187813866.785920.240410@i13g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<46CC290E.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`
- **References:** `<1187204587.545493.218790@q4g2000prc.googlegroups.com> <1187218735.434830.115180@m37g2000prh.googlegroups.com> <46C34C79.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <1187279407.749229.171320@x35g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <fa1ump$12j$1@reader1.panix.com> <1187287125.160229.308750@i13g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <5iks7rF3obe7oU1@mid.individual.net> <1187362496.040834.44620@i13g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <5in0hdF3qif6mU1@mid.individual.net> <1187723493.834715.99700@e9g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <5j1ma0F3s1j96U1@mid.individual.net> <46CC290E.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`

```

> Pete Dashwood wrote:
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> > the formal, indicates displeasure or hostility as far as I am concerned.

Frank Swarbrick wrote:
> Just want to say that I agree with this.  It is quite possible that people
> here are using "Mr so-and-so" here in a respectful manner, but my first
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

A brief story: One of my peers a while ago referred to me as
Grandpa :-) in an e-mail, and I took him to task for "taking
familiarities that were neither appropriate nor welcome."

I do believe that time once was the same view was held regarding use
of first names. Yes, I have friends, and I have business associates,
and sometimes they are the same, but sometimes not. The use of "Mr."
helps makes that distinction, albeit in a respectful way. To do
otherwise is to, ahem, "take liberties."

In another dialogue, in another forum, I challenged the use of an
exhortation that we "embrace our customers." I put forth the notion
that while I embrace many people, my wife, my family, and my friends,
I have never embraced my hardware salesman, or my accountant, or my
lawyer, or any number of people I do business with. Perhaps I was
blunt, to the point of being heavy-handed, when I continued: to do so
is a bit of a flirtation, and I am neither a panderer nor a harlot.
Though I appreciate my clients, I treat them with a certain, uh, arm's
length distance. My arm reaches far enough to shake their hand. That
will do.

A certain _Miss Manners_, aka Judith Martin, would be the arbiter of
protocol in this matters. Unfortunately, I do not have her _Miss
Manners Guide to Excruciatingly Correct Behavior_ with me right now.
But I know where a copy is, and eventually I will be able to give you
the final word, according to Miss Manners, on this issue.

Of one thing I am certain Miss Manners would agree with. Once
requested by an individual to be addressed in a certain way, it is
impolite not to do so.

Isn't that so, Pete and Frank? :-)

And OK, you two and I have a wee bit more than a nodding acquaintance,
so you may address me as "Ken". As for other CLC'ers, that familiarity
is to come only with the passing of some time, and the "paying of
dues", a concept which is hopefully not entirely outmoded.

As for addressing "the Doc", well you're all on your own, but somehow,
I feel safe with him. :-)

> Not sure what this mode of address has to do with going to an American
> College, though.
>

I am teaching Intermediate Algebra tonight at a community college. I
will address my students as Mr. and Ms. I expect to be addressed as
Mr. in return. I will make my effort to be kind and compassionate, as
well as respectful. Math is so intimidating for many of them.

Set operations, Union and Intersection, and Set Complementation, are
on the agenda, eventually. Shall I tell my students of De Morgan's
Laws? :-) You know, they apply to set operations as well as logical
booleans.

Later,
Ken
```

```
<klshafer@att.net> wrote in message 
news:1187813866.785920.240410@i13g2000prf.googlegroups.com...
>
>> Pete Dashwood wrote:
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes, "taking liberties" is to be avoided. However, my experience is that 
people take liberties no matter what form of address you use...:-)

I am not opposed to the old world views; all I can say is how it makes ME 
feel. I don't like it.

In todays world there is a real risk of coming across as pompous when 
formality is insisted upon.

(Nevertheless, there are times when formality is good...)

The test is in what makes people comfortable.

I believe true respect is in our interactions and not in the social 
machinery we use.

> In another dialogue, in another forum, I challenged the use of an
> exhortation that we "embrace our customers." I put forth the notion
> that while I embrace many people, my wife, my family, and my friends,
> I have never embraced my hardware salesman, or my accountant, or my
> lawyer, or any number of people I do business with.

Ah, Ken, you simply haven't lived... :-)   I always give my Tax Inspector a 
big hug before I go... :-)

I get a refund every year...not required for interviews any more...:-)

> Perhaps I was
> blunt, to the point of being heavy-handed, when I continued: to do so
> is a bit of a flirtation, and I am neither a panderer nor a harlot.

Shucks, me, I'm both... :-)

> Though I appreciate my clients, I treat them with a certain, uh, arm's
> length distance. My arm reaches far enough to shake their hand. That
> will do.
>

It's a nice statement. I can understand what you're saying, and enforced 
affability and "Hail fellow...well met" is quite sickening. I like to get to 
know my clients to the point where it isn't forced: I am genuinely 
interested in how they're doing and am glad to see them.

> A certain _Miss Manners_, aka Judith Martin, would be the arbiter of
> protocol in this matters. Unfortunately, I do not have her _Miss
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Isn't that so, Pete and Frank? :-)

Works for me :-)

>
> And OK, you two and I have a wee bit more than a nodding acquaintance,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> I feel safe with him. :-)

Aaarrrrrggggghhh!! He's fooled another one!!! :-)
>
>> Not sure what this mode of address has to do with going to an American
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> well as respectful. Math is so intimidating for many of them.

I get the feeling you are a very good teacher, Ken. The qualities you 
describe are conducive to good learning. Your students are lucky.

>
> Set operations, Union and Intersection, and Set Complementation, are
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The set operations of union and intersection correspond precisely with OR 
and AND. In fact, the Boolean Algebra can be derived very easily from set 
theory:  The sets become an "Algebra" when certain axioms and operations are 
fulfilled. Commutation, Distribution, Association, multiplicative identity 
(1), addititive identity (0)... Hey! Presto! Look Ma, I just invented an 
Algebra!!! :-) Although we usually talk of intersection or AND as 
"corresponding" to multiplication in normal Algebra, and union or OR as 
"corresponding" to addition, this is really just to give students some 
comfort with the idea of "operations" within an Algebra. The truth is that 
all of the aforementioned are simply defined "operations" within a set 
space.

(I better stop now before anybody realises I failed maths at school, hated 
it, was physically tortured by a female maths teacher who hated me (she made 
me sit apart from the class in a window, in the sun, until I nearly passed 
out , I was not allowed to move, and, in those days, you did as you were 
told...I was 11 at the time... hopefully, the sadistic bitch is dead 
now...:-)), and everything I know about Maths I taught myself after leaving 
school.

Motivation is a fantastic thing; I avoided Maths (did History and Social 
sciences instead) and managed to get through College without needing it. 
Then I found myself working in a place with scientists and chemists who 
needed computer programs and couldn't write them.
(How times have changed :-)... hard to imagine any scientist being 
non-computer-literate, today...)

 I learned enough Maths to understand what they were talking about and 
programmed what they needed. In 6 months working on my own at night (but 
with some help from the scientists I mentioned, who were very supportive of 
my efforts) I covered the 5 year High School syllabus and taught myself 
Calculus,  Probability Theory, and even the rudiments of things like 
topology, vector spaces, and non-Euclidean geometry. I loved it :-)   If 
anyone reading this is struggling with Maths, hang in there :-) When the 
need arises, you will rise to it, but it is far, far, better to avail 
yourself of the opportunity you have now, with tutors, other students and a 
support system.)

Pete.
```

```
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>person requests" (as a matter of etiquette) probably got right...

Kind of reminds me that many times when people use 'you' they seem to be 
intending 'me'; 'etiquette' - among other things - provides a framework 
for general social interactions so if it might be noted that a person does 
(x) for most folks ('generally') then (x) just might, possibly, be a form 
of etiquette.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Regarding EVALUATE TRUE

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-08-24T22:42:51+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5j7r1bF3t02rdU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<fa1ump$12j$1@reader1.panix.com> <5j436eF3snb1iU1@mid.individual.net> <kmgrc35v8335aq699btjt88j7er0c7jvjc@4ax.com> <5j6r7pF3ser2eU1@mid.individual.net> <fam7t2$aq$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```


<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:fam7t2$aq$1@reader1.panix.com...
> In article <5j6r7pF3ser2eU1@mid.individual.net>,
> Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> intending 'me';

Nope. Please feel excluded if my comment does not apply to you personally. 
"You" can be (as it was here) a less pompous form of the impersonal "one".

'etiquette' - among other things - provides a framework
> for general social interactions so if it might be noted that a person does
> (x) for most folks ('generally') then (x) just might, possibly, be a form
> of etiquette.
>

Sure. But if it isn't the form that makes people comfortable, then it is 
probably of little value. (It is certainly of little value to me).

Given that a person has expressed what they prefer, then compliance is most 
likely to make them feel comfortable. That particular etiquette is therefore 
probably the most valuable.

Depends on whether you want to be seen to be observing a particular 
etiquette, or whether you are seeking to interact comfortably with others. 
(Again, the "you" is impersonal and not directed at anyone in particular...I 
just can't bring myself to use "one", as I lived many years in London where 
this form is used by a certain "Class", with which I would not want to be 
associated.)

The reason you were excluded from my request, Doc, is because I know that 
YOU (this time it IS personal) feel more comfortable using that form. It 
doesn't make me feel comfortable and I would prefer you didn't, but I can 
live with it, as I have managed to do so for 10 years now. :-)

It would be unreasonable to expect you to change and I am not an 
unreasonable man. On the other hand, people who are newer here with less 
dues paid, I can point in the right direction before the rot sets in... :-)

It is precisely because of all these niceties and nuances that I suggested 
that respect really is in our interactions, not in our social machinery.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Regarding EVALUATE TRUE

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2007-08-24T12:45:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<famjtf$har$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<fa1ump$12j$1@reader1.panix.com> <5j6r7pF3ser2eU1@mid.individual.net> <fam7t2$aq$1@reader1.panix.com> <5j7r1bF3t02rdU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
In article <5j7r1bF3t02rdU1@mid.individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>Nope.

Mr Dashwood, this cannot be... are you saying that I cannot be reminded in 
this manner?  That's tantamount to denying me... freedom of association!

>Please feel excluded if my comment does not apply to you personally. 
>"You" can be (as it was here) a less pompous form of the impersonal "one".

Now there is what might be seen as an interesting equation... one form is 
'impersonal' and the alternative offered is 'less pompous'.  It can be 
asked whether this is an indication of how the language might be used in a 
situation or how an individual sees the language being used.

>
>'etiquette' - among other things - provides a framework
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>probably of little value. (It is certainly of little value to me).

Therein lies the rub, Mr Dashwood... does 'people' in your formulation 
here indicate only the subset those-who-are-addressed or larger set of 
those-who-are-addressed-AND-those-who-are-addressing?  Starting with the 
smallest group (a single person here not being considered as a 'group') 
one could readily observe:

A: 'I am comfortable when I address people as Mr/Ms.'
B: 'I am made comfortable when I am not addressed as Mr/Ms.'

... what form of interaction 'makes people comfortable'?

>
>Given that a person has expressed what they prefer, then compliance is most 
>likely to make them feel comfortable. That particular etiquette is therefore 
>probably the most valuable.

Ahhhhh... you've leapt from group-convention ('what makes people 
comfortable') to idiosyncratic preference('Given that a person...'); this 
can make for difficulties.

>Depends on whether you want to be seen to be observing a particular 
>etiquette, or whether you are seeking to interact comfortably with others. 

Addressed above... tangoing takes two and all that.  How can B interact 
comfortably with A if B is uncomfortable... and how can A interact with B 
if A is uncomfortable?

'After you, Alphonse.'
'Ah, no, no, no, no, no, no, no, no, no... after *you*, Gaston!'

>(Again, the "you" is impersonal and not directed at anyone in particular...I 
>just can't bring myself to use "one", as I lived many years in London where 
>this form is used by a certain "Class", with which I would not want to be 
>associated.)

Mr Dashwood, with all due respect... isn't this sort of snobbery 
antiquated?  Just because people are of a lower 'Class' than you does not 
mean you need to define yourself in reaction to their behaviors.

(oh... that 'Class' was not considered lower, but higher? that's all 
right, the preceding still seems to apply, just change the adjective; I 
believe something similar was pointed out in Austen's 'Pride and 
Prejudice')

[snip]

>It is precisely because of all these niceties and nuances that I suggested 
>that respect really is in our interactions, not in our social machinery.

Hmmmmm... so if it is 'in the Substance of the discussion and not the 
Form'... and the discussion turns to Formality then the Substance becomes 
one of Form...

(compare this with 'I hate spending money as a matter of principle... so, 
obviously, it's not a matter of money, it's a matter of principle!')

DD
```

- **Date:** 2007-08-24T08:24:14-06:00

- **Message-ID:** `<l8qtc3tijqjeddo49oqe2krhulrukas4ld@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<fa1ump$12j$1@reader1.panix.com> <5j436eF3snb1iU1@mid.individual.net> <kmgrc35v8335aq699btjt88j7er0c7jvjc@4ax.com> <5j6r7pF3ser2eU1@mid.individual.net> <fam7t2$aq$1@reader1.panix.com> <5j7r1bF3t02rdU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Fri, 24 Aug 2007 22:42:51 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>Given that a person has expressed what they prefer, then compliance is most 
>likely to make them feel comfortable. That particular etiquette is therefore 
>probably the most valuable.

Probably.    But further paying attention is warranted.    If the
person expressed a desire to be called X because of a temporary
condition - or for a desired relationship that is counter to your
goals, be ready to observe and adjust.

It's like having your 3rd grade teacher over for dinner with your
folks, or seeing her with her kids at the grocery store. Relationships
have to fit the environment.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Regarding EVALUATE TRUE

_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-08-25T12:13:26+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5j9ah8F3r84f7U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<fa1ump$12j$1@reader1.panix.com> <5j436eF3snb1iU1@mid.individual.net> <kmgrc35v8335aq699btjt88j7er0c7jvjc@4ax.com> <5j6r7pF3ser2eU1@mid.individual.net> <fam7t2$aq$1@reader1.panix.com> <5j7r1bF3t02rdU1@mid.individual.net> <l8qtc3tijqjeddo49oqe2krhulrukas4ld@4ax.com>`

```


"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:l8qtc3tijqjeddo49oqe2krhulrukas4ld@4ax.com...
> On Fri, 24 Aug 2007 22:42:51 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> have to fit the environment.

That's a very interesting consideration, Howard.

I read it several times and thought about it.

I can honestly say I have never "adjusted" a relationship with anyone, based 
on what they required as a form of address.

Neither can I agree that relationships have to fit the environment. 
(although I agree that an inappropriate relationship can be problematic in 
ANY environment :-))  What I CAN agree is that different kinds of 
relationship can be forged in different environments.

I would relate to my (hypothetical) third grade teacher in exactly the same 
way, whether she was having dinner at my parents house or whether I bumped 
into her at the supermarket.  If I like her and feel comfortable around her, 
that would be implicit in any environment.

The whole question of relationships is more complex than can be easily 
covered here.

Social machinery, manners, mores, is all a contrivance to help us relate to 
each other more easily.  But it is the tail, not the dog. I would not let 
someone's bad manners blind me to the intrinsic worth of that individual, 
although I'd probably have a quiet word later, and just mention that I found 
their boorishness hard to experience and would  appreciate it if they 
cleaned up their act :-) If they didn't, then I'd probably spend less time 
around them (I'm not a masochist :-)), but I wouldn't make judgements about 
them as a person, based on their lack of manners.

I believe it is important to differentiate between people and their 
behaviour. Good people (and I believe most people are basically good) can 
behave badly sometimes.

Last year I did my first trip to the Southern states of the USA. Georgia, 
Tennessee, mainly. I was very interested to observe the famous politeness of 
the South. I believe this is a case in point.  The people I met were warm 
and decent for the most part, but they definitely form opinions based on the 
prevailing "etiquette". After leaving and going to California where there is 
much less "finesse" :-) I couldn't decide which is "better".  Social 
machinery can mask shallowness in any culture, just as it can facilitate 
warmth and the growth of natural friendship.

The interaccions we have say much more about our true identity than any 
veneer of  manners or etiquette, but that doesn't mean that social machinery 
has no value or is worthless.

Pete.
```

```
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

I agree.   But don't assume that others don't check the tail wag to
form their opinion of you.


>Last year I did my first trip to the Southern states of the USA. Georgia, 
>Tennessee, mainly. I was very interested to observe the famous politeness of 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>warmth and the growth of natural friendship.

While there is the eternal observation by fogies that the young to not
show proper respect (they don't have our standards to the extent we
wish) - it is interesting to note how popular fiction is that shows
super polite bad guys.   I don't know how accurate Dickens was in
observing their behavior, but weren't those cutpurses polite!?!?
```

```
In article <1187813866.785920.240410@i13g2000prf.googlegroups.com>,
klshafer@att.net <klshafer@att.net> wrote:
>
>> Pete Dashwood wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>> impression is that the intention is to be insulting.

But soft... as has been told to folks new to the UseNet e'er-so-often, 
'first, you watch'; one needs to be careful of becoming the target of a 
Shavian (Caesar and Cleopatra) 'Pardon him. Theodotus: he is a barbarian, 
and thinks that the customs of his tribe and island are the laws of 
nature.'

>> With Doc I honestly
>> have no idea.  He's very hard to read.  :-)

How can this be?  I am speaking the finest of Englishes, all the doo-dah 
day!

[snip]

>I do believe that time once was the same view was held regarding use
>of first names. Yes, I have friends, and I have business associates,
>and sometimes they are the same, but sometimes not. The use of "Mr."
>helps makes that distinction, albeit in a respectful way. To do
>otherwise is to, ahem, "take liberties."

... and there you have it.  On the one hand to do as I do is to be cold, 
distant and excessively formal... on the other hand not doing as I do is 
overly familiar and libertarian... errrr, libertinage... errrrr, warm, 
close and insufficiently formal.

When in doubt about how a person's expressions are intended I do one of 
two things, usually: I ask... or I try to ignore the expressions and focus 
on what I see as the reasoning behind them. 

[snip]

>Of one thing I am certain Miss Manners would agree with. Once
>requested by an individual to be addressed in a certain way, it is
>impolite not to do so.

On the other hand... when one sees another using a form of address 
universally then to demand Special Treatment is also impolite... and 
perhaps it is possibly presumptuous, as well.  What to do?

[snip]

>As for addressing "the Doc", well you're all on your own, but somehow,
>I feel safe with him. :-)

Ahhhh... and now he is lulled into a false sense of security, his wariness 
made faint by my feints... drop into a crouch, pause, *lunge*... 
damnation, there's my bad leg again... will someone help me up, please?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Regarding EVALUATE TRUE

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2007-08-16T10:41:49-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<46C429ED.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`
- **References:** `<1187204587.545493.218790@q4g2000prc.googlegroups.com> <46C31C4C.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <1187218735.434830.115180@m37g2000prh.googlegroups.com> <46C34C79.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <1187279407.749229.171320@x35g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`

```
>>> On 8/16/2007 at 9:50 AM, in message
<1187279407.749229.171320@x35g2000prf.googlegroups.com>,
klshafer@att.net<klshafer@att.net> wrote:
> 
>      EVALUATE TRUE
…[75 more quoted lines elided]…
> much appreciate everyone who chimed in...

I think everything you have said makes sense.  As you mention, it probably
makes sense to put the WHEN-CASE-MATCHES-EXACTLY logic in its own
procedure.

I generally use EVALUATE TRUE when I am using 88 levels, where (yes) they
all refer to the same data item.  Which is most of the time, since I prefer
using 88 levels to using value codes.  I probably do have cases where I also
refer to 88 levels that *do not* refer to the same data item, but I can't
think of a specific example to give, or to even know whether it's justified
or not.  In the end I would say that your advice is wise, and worth
following as long as sensible exceptions can be made.

Thanks for a logical, sensible, well written post!

Frank
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Regarding EVALUATE TRUE

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2007-08-16T00:58:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fa07f6$aag$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<1187204587.545493.218790@q4g2000prc.googlegroups.com> <46C31C4C.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`

```
In article <46C31C4C.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>,
Frank Swarbrick <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> wrote:

[snip]

>I am curious on your opinion about the following:
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>'short-circuit' logic of EVALUATE.  I see nothing wrong with that.

I don't, either... then again, my vision might be faulty; in the Oldene 
Dayse I didn't seem anything wrong with NEXT SENTENCE, either.

[snip]

>Oh, and before anyone comments, yes I know I "missed" the "WHEN OTHER".  The
>fact is that ICMMSTR-HDR-OWIRE-LIM is required to always be numeric, so I
>could have just said "WHEN OTHER" for the last condition.  For whatever
>reason (I don't recall!) I chose to do it as shown above.

That 'whatever reason' might be 'because you have better habits than you 
give yourself credit for'.  Had you defaulted WHEN OTHER to be the 
equivalent of ICMMSTR-HDR-OWIRE-LIM NUMERIC then next month - or whenever 
such a thing happens - and another kind of Wire Out Limit is invented and 
has to be coded for there would be more work to do.

Address specific conditions specifically and future difficulties might be 
minimised.

>If I were to do
>it again I'd probably do WHEN OTHER, if only because it saves that final
>condition check.  Of course if that final value was not numeric I'd be in
>big trouble, but it is data retrieved from a file, and it was validated on
>input, so I think I'm pretty safe.

Leaving aside the ever-popular 'how much should one trust data?' I would 
argue that keeping the code clear is worth a couple-three CPU cycles every 
so often.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Regarding EVALUATE TRUE

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2007-08-15T20:22:45-06:00

- **Message-ID:** `<mqqdnUwbwpBjKV7bnZ2dnUVZ_hSdnZ2d@comcast.com>`

- **References:** `<1187204587.545493.218790@q4g2000prc.googlegroups.com> <46C31C4C.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <fa07f6$aag$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> 
> I don't, either... then again, my vision might be faulty; in the Oldene 
> Dayse I didn't seem anything wrong with NEXT SENTENCE, either.

Since this is an offshoot of a wandering thread anyway...  :)

Why do you think that the COBOL 85 standard did not define a clearer 
behavior for NEXT SENTENCE?  There has been a lot of confusion of it, 
and it seems that some of that could have been avoided had the standard 
defined its behavior in a different way.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Regarding EVALUATE TRUE

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2007-08-16T09:26:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fa158g$11r$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<1187204587.545493.218790@q4g2000prc.googlegroups.com> <46C31C4C.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <fa07f6$aag$1@reader1.panix.com> <mqqdnUwbwpBjKV7bnZ2dnUVZ_hSdnZ2d@comcast.com>`

```
In article <mqqdnUwbwpBjKV7bnZ2dnUVZ_hSdnZ2d@comcast.com>,
LX-i  <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>behavior for NEXT SENTENCE?

I barely know why *I* do things, Mr Somers, let alone anyone else.

>There has been a lot of confusion of it, 
>and it seems that some of that could have been avoided had the standard 
>defined its behavior in a different way.

As I posted then and - thus far - have no reason to view differently:  
from 
<http://groups.google.com/group/comp.lang.cobol/msg/542c3f1f0c6ec30b?dmode=source>

--begin quoted text:

As I was taught, lo, those many years ago: COBOL tries to be 
'English-like'.  A statement in English (usually) ends with a period/full 
stop.  NEXT SENTENCE, then, means 'skip everything between here and the 
next period and start up again after it (paragraph control aside)'.

--end quoted text

(Later in that thread Mr Simmons posted 'Thank you DOC.  I wanted to say 
that, but it's been so long since I understood all of current COBOL.'... 
and I responded then, and still believe now, that 'this compliment from 
you is one of the most meaningful I've received in my professional 
career.')

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Regarding EVALUATE TRUE

- **From:** "klshafer@att.net" <klshafer@att.net>
- **Date:** 2007-08-21T12:44:26-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1187725466.939948.219530@r23g2000prd.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<fa07f6$aag$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<1187204587.545493.218790@q4g2000prc.googlegroups.com> <46C31C4C.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <fa07f6$aag$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
On Aug 15, 8:58 pm, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:
> In article <46C31C4C.6F0F.008...@efirstbank.com>,
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

> That 'whatever reason' might be 'because you have better habits than you
> give yourself credit for'.  Had you defaulted WHEN OTHER to be the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> minimised.

I understand, and agree with Doc that Frank's initial inclination is
the better one. Not always, but many times, I prefer to be explicit on
the "last condition" to be evaluated, rather than let the WHEN OTHER
or the ELSE catch it. As was pointed out, bad input data (from a file,
or even a caller subprogram), or a runaway index/subscript can all
contribute to the seemingly "impossible", which then turns out to be
only "improbable."

Granted, this sometimes leads to difficult-to-test code (or maybe, not
"cost-effective to test every branch" code), like
EVALUATE TRUE
  WHEN permitted-condition-1-of-2
     <stuff>
  WHEN permitted-condition-2-of-2
     <stuff>
  WHEN OTHER
     DISPLAY "INTERNAL ERROR 1234 IN PARA XYZ"
     GO TO ABEND-EXIT
END-EVALUATE.

Nevertheless, the robustness displayed above has helped save my bacon,
or others', more than once.

And I do at least *try* to be reasonable. The useful limits to the
above are somewhat shy of ...

EVALUATE TRUE
   WHEN A=B
      <stuff>
   WHEN A<B
      <stuff>
   WHEN A>B
      <stuff>
   WHEN OTHER
     DISPLAY "CHECK FOR COMPILER ERROR"
     GO TO ABEND-EXIT
END-EVALUATE.

But for me, maybe not _very_ shy of it ;-) ... Sometimes my
"exhaustive" approach is "exhausting" to the patience of the One Who
Signs My Time Sheet, historically speaking... I have found it
particularly helpful in integration testing, however...

>
> Leaving aside the ever-popular 'how much should one trust data?' I would
> argue that keeping the code clear is worth a couple-three CPU cycles every
> so often.
>

Entering _all_ the conditions in the EVALUATE, instead of depending on
WHEN OTHER, also helps minimize page-flipping back to the list-of-88's
in Working-Storage to see what conditions constitute OTHER.

Thanks to Doc and Mr. Swarbrick for helping. I appreciate it.
Thanks again to everyone else who chose to comment. That also helps
and I appreciate that too.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Regarding EVALUATE TRUE

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2007-08-22T08:00:52-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gagoc39nout2dqgch02sea5e3rp6j4kmic@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1187204587.545493.218790@q4g2000prc.googlegroups.com> <46C31C4C.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <fa07f6$aag$1@reader1.panix.com> <1187725466.939948.219530@r23g2000prd.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Tue, 21 Aug 2007 12:44:26 -0700, "klshafer@att.net"
<klshafer@att.net> wrote:

>Entering _all_ the conditions in the EVALUATE, instead of depending on
>WHEN OTHER, also helps minimize page-flipping back to the list-of-88's
>in Working-Storage to see what conditions constitute OTHER.

Usually.    But sometimes WHEN OTHER is exactly what is being tested
for.
```

#### ↳ Re: Regarding EVALUATE TRUE

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2007-08-16T00:46:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fa06pg$ka1$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<1187204587.545493.218790@q4g2000prc.googlegroups.com>`

```
In article <1187204587.545493.218790@q4g2000prc.googlegroups.com>,
klshafer@att.net <klshafer@att.net> wrote:
>Usually I follow the Doc's prescription for whatever ails me in COBOL,
>as in...
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>is allowed by the ANSI '85 Standard.'

Eh?  Such praise would cause me to blush, Mr Shafer, were I able to 
remember how - it's been so long! - but that is not a 'prescription', it 
is a paraphrasing of interchanges I've had with folks who are confronted 
by some of the constructs I use.

>
>But allow me to put forth something about EVALUATE TRUE which causes
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>to a 2.00 power, ya know...

>
>Anyway, consider the following -
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>for the WHEN's and their imperatives.

Ahhhhhh... with all due respect, Mr Shafer, I would argue that this is a 
flaw of specs/design or implementation - not one inherent to EVALUATE - 
just as coding a check of two different data elements by IF/ELSE would be:

   IF TEMP > 80
       PERFORM 100-SWEAT
   ELSE 
       IF SKY-IS-BLUE (an 88 on IND-SKY)
           PERFORM 200-SMILE
       ELSE
           IF SNOW-IS-FALLING (an 88 on IND-SNOW)
               PERFORM 300-EXTEND-UMBRELLA
           ELSE
               PERFORM 400-STAY-HOME.

[snip]

>Now I'm OK with EVALUATE TRUE if care is taken to construct a clean
>and exhaustive WHEN partitioning... like...
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>END-EVALUATE

Some folks dislike 88s, Mr Shafer... me, I prefer them to PROCEDURE 
DIVISION hardcoded values.  Consider a classic:

EVALUATE TRUE
    WHEN WS-STATE = 'AL' OR 'AZ' OR 'AR'... OR 'WY'
        PERFORM A71352-CONUS-RTN   THRU  A71352-EX
    WHEN WS-STATE = 'AK' OR 'HI'
        PERFORM A73152-AK-OR-HI    THRU  A73152-EX
    WHEN WS-STATE = 'AS' OR 'GU' OR 'MH'... OR 'VI'
        PERFORM A75213-US-TERR     THRU  A75213-EX
    WHEN OTHER
        PERFORM A37512-INVALID-ST  THRU  A37512-EX
END-EVALUATE

...versus..

MOVE CUSTMAST-CUST-ST-RESIDNC  TO  WS-TEST-ST
EVALUATE TRUE
    WHEN LOWER-48
        PERFORM A71352-CONUS-RTN   THRU  A71352-EX
    WHEN ALASKA-OR-HAWAII
        PERFORM A73152-AK-OR-HI    THRU  A73152-EX
    WHEN US-TERRITORY
        PERFORM A75213-US-TERR     THRU  A75213-EX
    WHEN OTHER
        PERFORM A37512-INVALID-ST  THRU  A37512-EX
END-EVALUATE.

[snip]

>Yes, I know that the language definition of EVALUATE includes the
>order of the WHEN statements; but depending on the ordering in an
>EVALUATE just seems to me to be not in the spirit of what a "case"
>construct should be about.

What should be considered the case when using a 'case' construct, then, 
seems to be worthy of more than passing examination... LEFT-HANDED does 
not rule out BROWN-EYED does not rule out HAS-SIX-TOES.

>
>All in all, I think that it is a very bad idea to use EVALUATE TRUE
>with WHEN condition-1 and WHEN condition-2 and WHEN  condition-3 where
>condition-1, condition-2, and condition-3 refer to separate data-
>elements.

'Hey, it hurts when I do this.'

'Then don't do that!'

DD
```

```
>

…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
```
